title: Famine

infobox.value: is a widespread scarcity of [food](/wiki/Food "Food")[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) caused by several possible factors, including, but not limited to: [war](/wiki/War "War"), [natural disasters](/wiki/Natural_disasters "Natural disasters"), [crop failure](/wiki/Crop_failure "Crop failure"), widespread [poverty](/wiki/Poverty "Poverty"), an [economic catastrophe](/wiki/Financial_crisis "Financial crisis") or [government policies](/wiki/Government_policies "Government policies"). This phenomenon is usually accompanied or followed by regional [malnutrition](/wiki/Malnutrition "Malnutrition"), [starvation](/wiki/Starvation "Starvation"), [epidemic](/wiki/Epidemic "Epidemic"), and increased [mortality](/wiki/Death "Death"). Every inhabited continent in the world has experienced a period of famine throughout history. During the 19th and 20th centuries, [Southeast](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") and [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"), as well as [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") and [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe"), suffered the greatest number of fatalities due to famine. Deaths caused by famine declined sharply beginning in the 1970s, with numbers falling further since 2000. Since 2010, [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") has been the most affected continent in the world by famine. As of 2025, [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti") and [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") are the two countries with the most catastrophic and widespread states of famine, followed by [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Widespread scarcity of food

This article is about scarcity of food. For other uses, see [Famine (disambiguation)](/wiki/Famine_(disambiguation) "Famine (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/No-nb_bldsa_6a019.jpg/500px-No-nb_bldsa_6a019.jpg)](/wiki/File:No-nb_bldsa_6a019.jpg)

Two men and a child, all dead from starvation during the [Russian famine of 1921–1922](/wiki/Russian_famine_of_1921%E2%80%931922 "Russian famine of 1921–1922")

A **famine** is a widespread scarcity of [food](/wiki/Food "Food")[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) caused by several possible factors, including, but not limited to: [war](/wiki/War "War"), [natural disasters](/wiki/Natural_disasters "Natural disasters"), [crop failure](/wiki/Crop_failure "Crop failure"), widespread [poverty](/wiki/Poverty "Poverty"), an [economic catastrophe](/wiki/Financial_crisis "Financial crisis") or [government policies](/wiki/Government_policies "Government policies"). This phenomenon is usually accompanied or followed by regional [malnutrition](/wiki/Malnutrition "Malnutrition"), [starvation](/wiki/Starvation "Starvation"), [epidemic](/wiki/Epidemic "Epidemic"), and increased [mortality](/wiki/Death "Death"). Every inhabited continent in the world has experienced a period of famine throughout history. During the 19th and 20th centuries, [Southeast](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") and [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"), as well as [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") and [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe"), suffered the greatest number of fatalities due to famine. Deaths caused by famine declined sharply beginning in the 1970s, with numbers falling further since 2000. Since 2010, [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") has been the most affected continent in the world by famine. As of 2025, [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti") and [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") are the two countries with the most catastrophic and widespread states of famine, followed by [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan").

## Definitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definitions")]

According to the United Nations [World Food Programme](/wiki/World_Food_Programme "World Food Programme"), famine is declared when [malnutrition](/wiki/Malnutrition "Malnutrition") is widespread, and when people have started dying from starvation through lack of access to sufficient, nutritious food.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The [Integrated Food Security Phase Classification](/wiki/Integrated_Food_Security_Phase_Classification "Integrated Food Security Phase Classification") criteria define *Phase 5 famine* of acute food insecurity as occurring when all three of the following situations exist at the same time:[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-IPC_famine-5)

* At least 20% of households in an area face extreme food shortages with a limited ability to cope;
* The prevalence of acute malnutrition in children exceeds 30%;
* The death rate exceeds two people per 10,000 per day.

The declaration of a famine carries no binding obligations on the UN or member states, but serves to focus global attention on the problem.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [List of famines](/wiki/List_of_famines "List of famines")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Great_Tenmei_Famine_Map_Artwork.png/250px-Great_Tenmei_Famine_Map_Artwork.png)](/wiki/File:Great_Tenmei_Famine_Map_Artwork.png)

[Great Tenmei famine](/wiki/Great_Tenmei_famine "Great Tenmei famine") in Japan (1782–1788)

The cyclical occurrence of famine has been a mainstay of societies engaged in [subsistence agriculture](/wiki/Subsistence_agriculture "Subsistence agriculture") since the dawn of agriculture itself. The frequency and intensity of famine has fluctuated throughout history, depending on changes in food demand, such as [population growth](/wiki/Population_growth "Population growth"), and supply-side shifts caused by changing [climatic](/wiki/Climatic "Climatic") conditions.

### Decline of famine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Decline of famine")]

In the 16th and 17th centuries, the feudal system began to break down, and more prosperous farmers began to [enclose](/wiki/Enclosure "Enclosure") their own land and improve their yields to sell the surplus crops for a profit. These [capitalist](/wiki/Capitalist "Capitalist") landowners paid their labourers with [money](/wiki/Wage_labour "Wage labour"), thereby increasing the commercialization of rural society. In the emerging competitive labour market, better techniques for the improvement of labour productivity were increasingly valued and rewarded. It was in the farmer's interest to produce as much as possible on their land, in order to sell it to areas that demanded their produce. They produced guaranteed [surpluses](/wiki/Surplus_value "Surplus value") of their crop every year if they could.

Subsistence peasants were also increasingly forced to commercialize their activities because of increasing [taxes](/wiki/Tax "Tax"). Taxes that had to be paid to central governments in money, forced the peasants to produce crops intended for sale at market. This often meant producing [industrial crops](/wiki/Industrial_crop "Industrial crop") alongside subsistence crops, and forced peasants to increase their production in order to meet both their subsistence requirements as well as their tax obligations. Peasants also used the new money to purchase manufactured goods. The agricultural and social developments encouraging increased food production were gradually taking place throughout the 16th century, but took off in the early 17th century.

By the 1590s, these trends were sufficiently developed in the rich and commercialized province of [Holland](/wiki/Holland "Holland") to allow its population to withstand a general outbreak of famine in Western Europe at that time. By that time, the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") had one of the most commercialized agricultural systems in Europe. They grew many industrial crops such as [flax](/wiki/Flax "Flax"), [hemp](/wiki/Hemp "Hemp") and [hops](/wiki/Hops "Hops"). Agriculture became increasingly specialized and efficient. The efficiency of Dutch agriculture allowed for much more rapid urbanization in the late sixteenth and early seventeenth centuries than anywhere else in Europe. As a result, productivity and wealth increased, allowing the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") to maintain a steady food supply.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

By 1650, English agriculture had also become commercialized on a much wider scale. The last peacetime famine in England was in 1623–24. There were still periods of hunger, as in the Netherlands, but no more famines ever occurred. Common areas for pasture were [enclosed](/wiki/Enclosed "Enclosed") for private use and large scale, efficient farms were consolidated. Other technical developments included the draining of marshes, more efficient field use patterns, and the wider introduction of industrial crops. These agricultural developments led to wider prosperity in England and increasing urbanization.[[8]](#cite_note-8) By the end of the 17th century, English agriculture was the most productive in Europe.[[9]](#cite_note-9) In both England and the Netherlands, the population stabilized between 1650 and 1750, the same time period in which the sweeping changes to agriculture occurred. Famine still occurred in other parts of Europe, however. In [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"), famines occurred as late as the twentieth century.

### Attempts at famine alleviation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Attempts at famine alleviation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Skibbereen_by_James_Mahony%2C_1847.JPG/250px-Skibbereen_by_James_Mahony%2C_1847.JPG)](/wiki/File:Skibbereen_by_James_Mahony,_1847.JPG)

[Skibbereen](/wiki/Skibbereen "Skibbereen"), Ireland during the [Great Famine](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)"), 1847 illustration by [James Mahony](/wiki/James_Mahony "James Mahony") for the *[Illustrated London News](/wiki/Illustrated_London_News "Illustrated London News")*

In pre-industrial Europe preventing famine and ensuring timely food supplies was one of the chief concerns of many governments, as famines could lead to [revolt](/wiki/Revolt "Revolt") and other forms of social disruption. Most government they were severely limited in their options, though, due to limited levels of external trade, infrastructure, and bureaucracy generally too rudimentary to effect real relief.

By the mid-19th century and the onset of the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), it became possible for governments to alleviate the effects of famine through [price controls](/wiki/Price_controls "Price controls"), large scale importation of food products from foreign markets, stockpiling, [rationing](/wiki/Rationing "Rationing"), regulation of production and [charity](/wiki/Charity_(practice) "Charity (practice)"). The [Great Famine of 1845](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)") in Ireland was one of the first famines to feature such intervention, although the government response was often lackluster. The initial response of the British government to the early phase of the famine was "prompt and relatively successful", according to [F. S. L. Lyons](/wiki/F._S._L._Lyons "F. S. L. Lyons").[[10]](#cite_note-10) Confronted by widespread crop failure in the autumn of 1845, Prime Minister [Sir Robert Peel](/wiki/Sir_Robert_Peel "Sir Robert Peel") purchased £100,000 worth of maize and [cornmeal](/wiki/Cornmeal "Cornmeal") secretly from America. [Baring Brothers & Co](/wiki/Barings_Bank "Barings Bank") initially acted as purchasing agents for the Prime Minister. The government hoped that they would not "stifle private enterprise" and that their actions would not act as a disincentive to local relief efforts. Due to weather conditions, the first shipment did not arrive in Ireland until the beginning of February 1846.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKinealy199538-11) The maize corn was then re-sold for a penny a pound.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In 1846, Peel moved to repeal the [Corn Laws](/wiki/Corn_Laws "Corn Laws"), [tariffs](/wiki/Tariff "Tariff") on grain which kept the price of bread artificially high. The famine situation worsened during 1846 and the repeal of the Corn Laws in that year did little to help the starving Irish; the measure split the Conservative Party, leading to the fall of Peel's ministry.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlake1967221–241-13) In March, Peel set up a programme of public works in Ireland.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Famine_in_India_Natives_Waiting_for_Relief_in_Bangalore.jpg/250px-Famine_in_India_Natives_Waiting_for_Relief_in_Bangalore.jpg)](/wiki/File:Famine_in_India_Natives_Waiting_for_Relief_in_Bangalore.jpg)

People waiting for famine relief in [Bangalore](/wiki/Bangalore "Bangalore"), India (from the *[Illustrated London News](/wiki/Illustrated_London_News "Illustrated London News")*, 1877)

Despite this promising start, the measures undertaken by Peel's successor, [Lord John Russell](/wiki/Lord_John_Russell "Lord John Russell"), proved comparatively "inadequate" as the crisis deepened. Russell's ministry introduced public works projects, which by December 1846 employed some half million Irish and proved impossible to administer. The government was influenced by a [laissez-faire](/wiki/Laissez-faire "Laissez-faire") belief that the market would provide the food needed. It halted government food and relief works, and turned to a mixture of "indoor" and "outdoor" direct relief; the former administered in [workhouses](/wiki/Workhouse "Workhouse") through the [Poor Law](/wiki/Irish_Poor_Laws "Irish Poor Laws"), the latter through [soup kitchens](/wiki/Soup_kitchen "Soup kitchen").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Apocalypse_vasnetsov.jpg/330px-Apocalypse_vasnetsov.jpg)](/wiki/File:Apocalypse_vasnetsov.jpg)

*[Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse](/wiki/Four_Horsemen_of_the_Apocalypse_(painting) "Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse (painting)")*, an 1887 painting by Russian artist [Viktor Vasnetsov](/wiki/Viktor_Vasnetsov "Viktor Vasnetsov"). Depicted from left to right are Death, Famine, War, and Conquest.

A systematic attempt at creating the necessary regulatory framework for dealing with famine was developed by the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") in the 1880s. In order to comprehensively address the issue of famine, the British created an Indian Famine commission to recommend steps that the government would be required to take in the event of a famine.[[16]](#cite_note-L._Brennan-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Britannica-17)[[18]](#cite_note-Scales-18) The Famine Commission issued a series of government guidelines and regulations on how to respond to famines and food shortages called the Famine Code. The famine code was also one of the first attempts to scientifically predict famine in order to mitigate its effects. These were finally passed into law in 1883 under [Lord Ripon](/wiki/Lord_Ripon "Lord Ripon").

The [Code](/wiki/Indian_Famine_Codes "Indian Famine Codes") introduced the first [famine scale](/wiki/Famine_scale "Famine scale"): three levels of [food insecurity](/wiki/Food_insecurity "Food insecurity") were defined: near-scarcity, scarcity, and famine. "Scarcity" was defined as three successive years of [crop failure](/wiki/Crop_failure "Crop failure"), [crop yields](/wiki/Crop_yield "Crop yield") of one-third or one-half normal, and large populations in distress. "Famine" further included a rise in food prices above 140% of "normal", the movement of people in search of food, and widespread mortality.[[16]](#cite_note-L._Brennan-16) The Commission identified that the loss of wages from lack of employment of agricultural labourers and artisans were the cause of famines. The Famine Code applied a strategy of generating employment for these sections of the population and relied on open-ended public works to do so.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 20th century")]

During the 20th century, an estimated 70 to 120 million people died from [famines across the world](/wiki/List_of_famines "List of famines"), of whom over half died in China, with an estimated 30 million dying during the [famine of 1958–1961](/wiki/Great_Chinese_Famine "Great Chinese Famine"),[[20]](#cite_note-IDSWorkingPaper-20) up to 10 million in the [Chinese famine of 1928–1930](/wiki/Chinese_famine_of_1928%E2%80%931930 "Chinese famine of 1928–1930"), and over two million in the [Chinese famine of 1942–1943](/wiki/Chinese_famine_of_1942%E2%80%931943 "Chinese famine of 1942–1943"), and millions more lost in famines in North and East China. The USSR lost 8 million claimed by the [Soviet famine of 1930–1933](/wiki/Soviet_famine_of_1930%E2%80%931933 "Soviet famine of 1930–1933"), over a million in both the [Soviet famine of 1946–1947](/wiki/Soviet_famine_of_1946%E2%80%931947 "Soviet famine of 1946–1947") and [Siege of Leningrad](/wiki/Siege_of_Leningrad "Siege of Leningrad"), the 5 million in the [Russian famine of 1921–1922](/wiki/Russian_famine_of_1921%E2%80%931922 "Russian famine of 1921–1922"), and others famines. Java suffered 2.5 million deaths under Japanese occupation during World War Two.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The other most notable famine of the century was the [Bengal famine of 1943](/wiki/Bengal_famine_of_1943 "Bengal famine of 1943"), resulting both from the [Japanese occupation of Burma](/wiki/Japanese_occupation_of_Burma "Japanese occupation of Burma"), resulting in an influx of refugees, and blocking Burmese grain imports and a failure of the [Bengali provincial Government](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency#Provincial_Autonomy "Bengal Presidency") to [declare a famine](/wiki/Indian_Famine_Codes "Indian Famine Codes"), and fund relief, the imposition of grain and transport embargoes by the neighbouring provincial administrations, to prevent their own stocks being transferred to Bengal, the failure to implement India wide rationing by the central Delhi authority, hoarding and profiteering by [merchants](/wiki/Merchants "Merchants"), medieval land management practices, an Axis powers denial program that confiscated boats once used to transport grain, a Delhi administration that prioritised supplying, and offering medical treatment to the British Indian Army, War workers, and Civil servants, over the populace at large, incompetence and ignorance, and an [Imperial War Cabinet](/wiki/Churchill_war_ministry "Churchill war ministry") initially leaving the issue to the Colonial administration to resolve, than to the original local crop failures, and blights.[[22]](#cite_note-Sen-22)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Bengal_famine_1943_photo.jpg/250px-Bengal_famine_1943_photo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bengal_famine_1943_photo.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Ondervoed_kindje_hongerwinter.jpg/120px-Ondervoed_kindje_hongerwinter.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ondervoed_kindje_hongerwinter.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/1968_5Nigeria_CDC.png/250px-1968_5Nigeria_CDC.png)](/wiki/File:1968_5Nigeria_CDC.png)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Irish_potato_famine_Bridget_O%27Donnel.jpg/250px-Irish_potato_famine_Bridget_O%27Donnel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Irish_potato_famine_Bridget_O%27Donnel.jpg)

From top-left to bottom-right, or (mobile) from top-to-bottom: child victims of famines in
[India (1943–44)](/wiki/Bengal_famine_of_1943 "Bengal famine of 1943"), [the Netherlands (1944–45)](/wiki/Dutch_famine_of_1944%E2%80%9345 "Dutch famine of 1944–45"), [Nigeria (1967–70)](/wiki/Nigerian_Civil_War "Nigerian Civil War"), and an engraving of a woman and her children during the [Great Famine in Ireland (1845–1849)](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)")

A few of the great famines of the late 20th century were: the [Biafran famine](/wiki/Biafra "Biafra") in the 1960s, the [Khmer Rouge](/wiki/Khmer_Rouge "Khmer Rouge")-caused famine in Cambodia in the 1970s, the North Korean [famine of the 1990s](/wiki/North_Korean_famine "North Korean famine"), and the Ethiopian [famine of 1983–1985](/wiki/1983%E2%80%931985_famine_in_Ethiopia "1983–1985 famine in Ethiopia"). Approximately 3 million died as a consequence of the [Second Congo War](/wiki/Second_Congo_War "Second Congo War").

The Ethiopian famine was reported on television reports around the world, carrying footage of starving Ethiopians whose plight was centered around a feeding station near the town of [Korem](/wiki/Korem "Korem"). This stimulated the first mass movements to end famine across the world.

[BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") newsreader [Michael Buerk](/wiki/Michael_Buerk "Michael Buerk") gave moving commentary of the tragedy on 23 October 1984, which he described as a "biblical famine". This prompted the [Band Aid](/wiki/Band_Aid_(band) "Band Aid (band)") single, which was organized by [Bob Geldof](/wiki/Bob_Geldof "Bob Geldof") and featured more than 20 pop stars. The [Live Aid](/wiki/Live_Aid "Live Aid") concerts in [London](/wiki/London "London") and [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") raised even more funds for the cause. Hundreds of thousands of people died within one year as a result of the famine, but the publicity Live Aid generated encouraged Western nations to make available enough surplus grain to end the immediate hunger crisis in Africa.[[23]](#cite_note-History-23)

Some of the famines of the 20th century served the geopolitical purposes of governments, including traumatizing and replacing distrusted ethnic populations in strategically important regions, rendering regions vulnerable to invasion difficult to govern by an enemy power and shifting the burden of food shortage onto regions where the distress of the population posed a lesser risk of catastrophic regime de-legitimation.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 21st century")]

See also: [COVID-19 pandemic-related famines](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic-related_famines "COVID-19 pandemic-related famines") and [2022–2023 food crises](/wiki/2022%E2%80%932023_food_crises "2022–2023 food crises")

Until 2017, worldwide deaths from famine had been falling dramatically. The [World Peace Foundation](/wiki/World_Peace_Foundation "World Peace Foundation") reported that from the 1870s to the 1970s, great famines killed an average of 928,000 people a year.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Since 1980, annual deaths had dropped to an average of 75,000, less than 10% of what they had been until the 1970s. That reduction was achieved despite the approximately 150,000 lives lost in the [2011 Somalia famine](/wiki/2011_Somalia_famine "2011 Somalia famine"). Yet in 2017, the UN officially declared famine had returned to Africa, with about 20 million people at risk of death from starvation in the northern part of Nigeria, in [South Sudan](/wiki/2017_South_Sudan_famine "2017 South Sudan famine"), in [Yemen](/wiki/Famine_in_Yemen "Famine in Yemen"), and in [Somalia](/wiki/2017_Somalian_drought "2017 Somalian drought").[[26]](#cite_note-democracyFT-26)

On 20 April 2021, hundreds of aid organizations from around the world wrote an open letter to [The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian") newspaper, warning that millions of people in [Yemen](/wiki/Yemen "Yemen"), [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia"), [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan"), [Burkina Faso](/wiki/Burkina_Faso "Burkina Faso"), [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo"), [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras "Honduras"), [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela"), [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria"), [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti"), [Central African Republic](/wiki/Central_African_Republic "Central African Republic"), [Uganda](/wiki/Uganda "Uganda"), [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe") and [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan") faced starvation. Organizations including the International Council of Voluntary Agencies and the [World Food Programme](/wiki/World_Food_Programme "World Food Programme") said: "Girls and boys, men and women, are being starved by conflict and violence; by inequality; by the impacts of climate change; by the loss of land, jobs of prospects; by a fight against Covid-19 that has left them even further behind". The groups warned that funding had dwindled, while money alone would not be enough by itself. Governments should step in to end conflicts and ensure humanitarian access, they said. "If no action is taken, lives will be lost. The responsibility to address this lies with states", they added.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In November 2021, the [World Food Programme](/wiki/World_Food_Programme "World Food Programme") reported that 45 million people were "teetering on the very edge of famine" in 43 countries and that the slightest shock would push them over the precipice. This number had risen from 42 million earlier in 2021, and from 27 million in 2019.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The slightest shock — be it extreme weather linked to climate change, conflict, or the deadly interplay of both hunger drivers — could push tens of millions of people into irreversible peril, a prospect the agency had been warning of for more than a year. [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") was becoming the world's largest humanitarian crisis, with the country's needs surpassing those of the other worst-hit countries — [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia"), [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan"), [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria") and even [Yemen](/wiki/Yemen "Yemen").[[29]](#cite_note-29)

In August 2024, [famine](/wiki/2024_famine_in_Sudan "2024 famine in Sudan") was declared in Sudan,[[30]](#cite_note-30) where several refugee camps, displaced by the ongoing [Sudanese civil war](/wiki/Sudanese_civil_war_(2023%E2%80%93present) "Sudanese civil war (2023–present)"), were found to be facing severe famine conditions. According to the [IPC](/wiki/Integrated_Food_Security_Phase_Classification "Integrated Food Security Phase Classification"), over 24 million people are facing acute food insecurity, and a phase 5 famine was detected in multiple regions.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) UN experts have accused the [Sudanese Armed Forces](/wiki/Sudanese_Armed_Forces "Sudanese Armed Forces") (SAF) and the [Rapid Support Forces](/wiki/Rapid_Support_Forces "Rapid Support Forces") (RSF) of using 'starvation tactics' against the civilians in the country, and have said "Never in modern history have so many people faced starvation and famine as in [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan") today".[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In August 2025, [famine](/wiki/Gaza_Strip_famine "Gaza Strip famine") was declared in Gaza,[[34]](#cite_note-34) More than half a million people in Gaza were trapped in famine, marked by widespread starvation, destitution and preventable deaths, according to a new Integrated Food Security Phase Classification (IPC) analysis that was released,[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-:0-36) Access to food in Gaza remains severely constrained[[37]](#cite_note-37). In July, the number of households reporting very severe hunger doubled across the territory compared to May and more than tripled in Gaza City. More than one in three people (39 percent) indicated they were going days at a time without eating, and adults regularly skip meals to feed their children.

Malnutrition among children in Gaza is accelerating at a catastrophic pace. In July alone, more than 12,000 children were identified as acutely malnourished – the highest monthly figure ever recorded and a six-fold increase since the start of the year. Nearly one in four of these children were suffering from severe acute malnutrition (SAM), the deadliest form with both short and long-term impacts.[[36]](#cite_note-:0-36)

According to the IPC Analysis in May, the number of children expected to be at severe risk of death from malnutrition by the end of June 2026 has tripled from 14,100 to 43,400. Similarly, for pregnant and breastfeeding women, the number of estimated cases has tripled from 17,000 in May to 55,000 women expected to be suffering from perilous levels of malnutrition by mid-2026. The impact is visible: one in five babies are born prematurely or underweight.[[36]](#cite_note-:0-36)

The new assessment reports the most severe deterioration since the IPC began analyzing acute food insecurity and acute malnutrition in the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip"), and it marks the first time a famine has been officially confirmed in the Middle East region.[[36]](#cite_note-:0-36)

Meanwhile, approximately 98 percent of cropland in the territory is damaged or inaccessible – decimating the agriculture sector and local food production – and nine of ten people have been serially displaced from homes. Cash is critically scarce, aid operations remain severely disrupted, with most UN trucks looted amid growing desperation. Food prices are extremely high and there is not enough fuel and water to cook and medicines and medical supplies.[[36]](#cite_note-:0-36)

Gaza's health system has severely deteriorated, access to safe drinking water and sanitation services has been drastically reduced, while multi-drug resistant infections are surging and levels of morbidity – including diarrhea, fever, acute respiratory and skin infections – are alarmingly high among children.[[36]](#cite_note-:0-36)

### Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Africa")]

See also: [Category:Famines in Africa](/wiki/Category:Famines_in_Africa "Category:Famines in Africa")

#### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Early history")]

In the mid-22nd century BC, a sudden and short-lived climatic change that caused reduced rainfall resulted in several decades of drought in [Upper Egypt](/wiki/Upper_Egypt "Upper Egypt"). The resulting famine and civil strife is believed to have been a major cause of the collapse of the [Old Kingdom](/wiki/Old_Kingdom "Old Kingdom"). An account from the [First Intermediate Period](/wiki/First_Intermediate_Period "First Intermediate Period") states, "All of Upper Egypt was dying of hunger and people were eating their children." As for recorded examples about more recent centuries: in the 1680s, famine extended across the entire [Sahel](/wiki/Sahel "Sahel"), and in 1738 half the population of [Timbuktu](/wiki/Timbuktu "Timbuktu") died of famine.[[38]](#cite_note-38) In [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), between 1687 and 1731, there were six famines.[[39]](#cite_note-39) The famine that afflicted Egypt in 1784 cost it roughly one-sixth of its population.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The [Maghreb](/wiki/Maghreb "Maghreb") experienced famine and [plague](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death") in the late 18th century and early 19th century.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) There was famine in [Tripoli](/wiki/Tripoli,_Libya "Tripoli, Libya") in 1784, and in [Tunis](/wiki/Tunis "Tunis") in 1785.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

According to John Iliffe, "Portuguese records of [Angola](/wiki/Angola "Angola") from the 16th century show that a great famine occurred on average every seventy years; accompanied by epidemic disease, it might kill one-third or one-half of the population, destroying the demographic growth of a generation and forcing colonists back into the river valleys."[[44]](#cite_note-44)

The first documentation of weather in West-Central Africa occurred around the mid-16th to 17th centuries in areas such as Luanda Kongo, however, not much data was recorded on the issues of weather and disease except for a few notable documents. The only records obtained are of violence between Portuguese and Africans during the [Battle of Mbwila](/wiki/Battle_of_Mbwila "Battle of Mbwila") in 1665. In these documents the Portuguese wrote of African raids on Portuguese merchants solely for food, giving clear signs of famine. Additionally, instances of [cannibalism](/wiki/Human_cannibalism "Human cannibalism") by the African Jaga were also more prevalent during this time frame, indicating an extreme deprivation of a primary food source.[[45]](#cite_note-Miller_17–61-45)

#### Colonial period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Colonial period")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Punch_congo_rubber_cartoon.jpg/250px-Punch_congo_rubber_cartoon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Punch_congo_rubber_cartoon.jpg)

A 1906 *[Punch](/wiki/Punch_(magazine) "Punch (magazine)")* cartoon depicting [King Leopold II](/wiki/King_Leopold_II "King Leopold II") as a snake entangling a Congolese man

A notable period of famine occurred around the turn of the 20th century in the [Congo Free State](/wiki/Congo_Free_State "Congo Free State"). In forming this state, Leopold used mass labor camps to finance his empire.[[46]](#cite_note-Masci_2002-46) This period resulted in the death of up to 10 million Congolese from brutality, disease and famine.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Some colonial "pacification" efforts often caused severe famine, notably with the repression of the Maji Maji revolt in [Tanganyika](/wiki/German_East_Africa "German East Africa") in 1906. The introduction of cash crops such as cotton, and forcible measures to impel farmers to grow these crops, sometimes impoverished the peasantry in many areas, such as northern Nigeria, contributing to greater vulnerability to famine when severe drought struck in 1913.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

A large-scale famine occurred in Ethiopia in 1888 and in succeeding years, as the [rinderpest epizootic](/wiki/1890s_African_rinderpest_epizootic "1890s African rinderpest epizootic"), introduced into [Eritrea](/wiki/Eritrea "Eritrea") by infected cattle, spread southwards reaching ultimately as far as [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"). In Ethiopia it was estimated that as much as 90 percent of the national herd died, rendering rich farmers and herders destitute overnight. This coincided with [drought](/wiki/Drought "Drought") associated with an *[El Niño](/wiki/El_Ni%C3%B1o "El Niño")* oscillation, human epidemics of [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox"), and in several countries, intense war. The [Ethiopian Great famine](/wiki/Famines_in_Ethiopia "Famines in Ethiopia") that afflicted Ethiopia from 1888 to 1892 cost it roughly one-third of its population.[[49]](#cite_note-49) In [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan") the year 1888 is remembered as the worst famine in history, on account of these factors and also the [exactions](/wiki/Exaction "Exaction") imposed by the [Mahdist state](/wiki/Muhammad_Ahmad "Muhammad Ahmad").

The [oral traditions](/wiki/Oral_tradition "Oral tradition") of the [Himba people](/wiki/Himba_people "Himba people") recall two droughts from 1910 to 1917. From 1910 to 1911 the Himba described the drought as "drought of the omutati seed", also called *omangowi*, the fruit of an unidentified vine that people ate during the time period. From 1914 to 1916, droughts brought *katur' ombanda* or *kari' ombanda* 'the time of eating clothing'.[[45]](#cite_note-Miller_17–61-45)

#### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/e/e1/Niger_childhood_malnutrition_16oct06.jpg)](/wiki/File:Niger_childhood_malnutrition_16oct06.jpg)

Malnourished children in [Niger](/wiki/Niger "Niger"), during the 2005 famine

For the middle part of the 20th century, agriculturalists, economists and geographers did not consider Africa to be especially famine prone. From 1870 to 2010, 87% of deaths from famine occurred in Asia and Eastern Europe, with only 9.2% in Africa.[[26]](#cite_note-democracyFT-26) There were notable counter-examples, such as the famine in [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda") during World War II and the [Malawi](/wiki/Malawi "Malawi") famine of 1949, but most famines were localized and brief food shortages. Although the drought was brief the main cause of death in Rwanda was due to Belgian prerogatives to acquisition grain from their colony (Rwanda). The increased grain acquisition was related to WW2. This and the drought caused 300,000 Rwandans to perish.[[46]](#cite_note-Masci_2002-46)

From 1967 to 1969 large scale famine occurred in Biafra and Nigeria due to a [government blockade of the Breakaway territory](/wiki/Nigerian_Civil_War "Nigerian Civil War"). It is estimated that 1.5 million people died of starvation due to this famine. Additionally, drought and other government interference with the food supply caused 500 thousand Africans to perish in Central and West Africa.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

Famine recurred in the early 1970s, when Ethiopia and the west African [Sahel](/wiki/Sahel "Sahel") suffered [drought and famine](/wiki/Sahel_drought "Sahel drought"). The Ethiopian famine of that time was closely linked to the crisis of feudalism in that country, and in due course helped to bring about the downfall of the Emperor [Haile Selassie](/wiki/Haile_Selassie "Haile Selassie"). The Sahelian famine was associated with the slowly growing crisis of pastoralism in Africa, which has seen livestock herding decline as a viable way of life over the last two generations.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Starved_girl.jpg/250px-Starved_girl.jpg)](/wiki/File:Starved_girl.jpg)

A girl during the [Nigerian Civil War](/wiki/Nigerian_Civil_War "Nigerian Civil War") of the late 1960s. Pictures of the famine caused by the Nigerian blockade garnered sympathy for the Biafrans worldwide.

Famines occurred in Sudan in the late-1970s and again in 1990 and 1998. The 1980 famine in [Karamoja](/wiki/Karamoja "Karamoja"), [Uganda](/wiki/Uganda "Uganda") was, in terms of mortality rates, one of the worst in history. 21% of the population died, including 60% of the infants.[[51]](#cite_note-51) In the 1980s, large scale multilayer drought occurred in the Sudan and Sahelian regions of Africa. This caused famine because even though the Sudanese Government believed there was a surplus of grain, there were local deficits across the region.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

In October 1984, television reports describing the Ethiopian famine as "biblical", prompted the [Live Aid](/wiki/Live_Aid "Live Aid") concerts in London and Philadelphia, which raised large sums to alleviate the suffering. A primary cause of the famine (one of the largest seen in the country) is that Ethiopia (and the surrounding Horn) was still recovering from the droughts which occurred in the mid-late 1970s. Compounding this problem was the intermittent fighting due to [Ethiopian Civil War](/wiki/Ethiopian_Civil_War "Ethiopian Civil War"), the [government](/wiki/Derg "Derg")'s lack of organization in providing relief, and hoarding of supplies to control the population. Ultimately, over 1 million Ethiopians died and over 22 million people suffered due to the prolonged drought, which lasted roughly 2 years.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

In 1992 Somalia became a war zone with no effective government, police, or basic services after the collapse of the dictatorship led by [Siad Barre](/wiki/Siad_Barre "Siad Barre") and the split of power between warlords. This coincided with a massive drought, causing over 300,000 Somalis to perish.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

#### Recent years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Recent years")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/8a/Maradi_aidecentre_Niger9aug2005_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maradi_aidecentre_Niger9aug2005_2.jpg)

Laure Souley holds her three-year-old daughter and an infant son at a MSF aide center during the 2005 famine, Maradi Niger

Since the start of the 21st century, more effective early warning and humanitarian response actions have reduced the number of deaths by famine markedly. That said, many African countries are not self-sufficient in food production, relying on income from [cash crops](/wiki/Cash_crop "Cash crop") to import food. [Agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture") in Africa is susceptible to [climatic](/wiki/Climatic "Climatic") fluctuations, especially [droughts](/wiki/Drought "Drought") which can reduce the amount of food produced locally. Other agricultural problems include [soil infertility](/wiki/Soil_fertility "Soil fertility"), [land degradation](/wiki/Land_degradation "Land degradation") and [erosion](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion"), swarms of [desert locusts](/wiki/Desert_locust "Desert locust"), which can destroy whole crops, and livestock diseases. [Desertification](/wiki/Desertification "Desertification") is increasingly problematic: the [Sahara](/wiki/Sahara "Sahara") reportedly spreads up to 48 kilometres (30 mi) per year.[[55]](#cite_note-55) The most serious famines have been caused by a combination of drought, misguided economic policies, and conflict. The 1983–85 famine in Ethiopia, for example, was the outcome of all these three factors, made worse by the Communist government's censorship of the emerging crisis. In Capitalist Sudan at the same date, drought and economic crisis combined with denials of any food shortage by the then-government of President [Gaafar Nimeiry](/wiki/Gaafar_Nimeiry "Gaafar Nimeiry"), to create a crisis that killed perhaps 250,000 people—and helped bring about a popular uprising that overthrew Nimeiry.

Numerous factors make the [food security](/wiki/Food_security "Food security") situation in Africa tenuous, including political instability, armed conflict and [civil war](/wiki/Civil_war "Civil war"), [corruption](/wiki/Political_corruption "Political corruption") and mismanagement in handling food supplies, and trade policies that harm African agriculture. An example of a famine created by human rights abuses is the [1998 Sudan famine](/wiki/1998_Sudan_famine "1998 Sudan famine"). [AIDS](/wiki/AIDS "AIDS") is also having long-term economic effects on agriculture by reducing the available workforce, and is creating new vulnerabilities to famine by overburdening poor households. On the other hand, in the modern history of Africa on quite a few occasions famines acted as a major source of acute political instability.[[56]](#cite_note-56) In Africa, if current trends of [population growth](/wiki/Population_growth "Population growth") and [soil degradation](/wiki/Soil_degradation "Soil degradation") continue, the continent might be able to feed just 25% of its population by 2025, according to [United Nations University](/wiki/United_Nations_University "United Nations University") (UNU)'s Ghana-based Institute for Natural Resources in Africa.[[57]](#cite_note-news.mongabay.com-57)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Sahel_Map-Africa_rough.png/250px-Sahel_Map-Africa_rough.png)](/wiki/File:Sahel_Map-Africa_rough.png)

Famine-affected areas in the western [Sahel](/wiki/Sahel "Sahel") belt during the [2012 drought](/wiki/2012_Sahel_drought "2012 Sahel drought").

Famines in the early 21st century in Africa include the [2005–06 Niger food crisis](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_Niger_food_crisis "2005–06 Niger food crisis"), the [2010 Sahel famine](/wiki/2010_Sahel_famine "2010 Sahel famine") and the [2011 East Africa drought](/wiki/2011_East_Africa_drought "2011 East Africa drought"), where two consecutive missed rainy seasons precipitated one of the worst [droughts in East Africa](/wiki/2011_East_Africa_drought "2011 East Africa drought") in 60 years.[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-Twdisyihoa-59) An estimated 50,000 to 150,000 people are reported to have died during the period.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-Mafhscbafro-61) In 2012, the [Sahel drought](/wiki/2012_Sahel_drought "2012 Sahel drought") put more than 10 million people in the western Sahel at risk of famine (according to a [Methodist Relief & Development Fund (MRDF)](/wiki/Methodist_Relief_%26_Development_Fund_(MRDF) "Methodist Relief & Development Fund (MRDF)") aid expert), due to a month-long heat wave.[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-http://www.poverties.org_Famine_in_Africa-63)

Today, famine is most widespread in [Sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa"), but with exhaustion of food resources, overdrafting of [groundwater](/wiki/Groundwater "Groundwater"), wars, internal struggles, and economic failure, famine continues to be a worldwide problem with hundreds of millions of people suffering.[[64]](#cite_note-64) These famines cause widespread malnutrition and impoverishment. The [famine in Ethiopia](/wiki/1984%E2%80%931985_famine_in_Ethiopia "1984–1985 famine in Ethiopia") in the 1980s had an immense death toll, although Asian famines of the 20th century have also produced extensive death tolls. Modern African famines are characterized by widespread destitution and malnutrition, with heightened mortality confined to young children.

#### Current initiatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Current initiatives")]

Against a backdrop of conventional interventions through the state or markets, alternative initiatives have been pioneered to address the problem of food security. One pan-African example is the [Great Green Wall](/wiki/Great_Green_Wall_(Africa) "Great Green Wall (Africa)"). Another example is the "Community Area-Based Development Approach" to agricultural development ("CABDA"), an NGO programme with the objective of providing an alternative approach to increasing food security in Africa. CABDA proceeds through specific areas of intervention such as the introduction of drought-resistant crops and new methods of food production such as agro-forestry. Piloted in Ethiopia in the 1990s it has spread to Malawi, Uganda, Eritrea and Kenya. In an analysis of the programme by the Overseas Development Institute, CABDA's focus on individual and community capacity-building is highlighted. This enables farmers to influence and drive their own development through community-run institutions, bringing food security to their household and region.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

#### The role of African Unity organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: The role of African Unity organization")]

The organization of African unity and its role in the African crisis has been interested in the political aspects of the continent, especially the liberation of the occupied parts of it and the elimination of racism. The organization has succeeded in this area, but has not achieved success in the economic field or in development. African leaders have agreed to waive the role of their organization in the development to the United Nations through the Economic Commission for Africa "ECA".[[66]](#cite_note-66)

### Far East

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Far East")]

#### China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: China")]

See also: [List of famines in China](/wiki/List_of_famines_in_China "List of famines in China"), [Northern Chinese Famine of 1876–1879](/wiki/Northern_Chinese_Famine_of_1876%E2%80%931879 "Northern Chinese Famine of 1876–1879"), [Chinese famine of 1928–1930](/wiki/Chinese_famine_of_1928%E2%80%931930 "Chinese famine of 1928–1930"), and [Chinese famine of 1942–1943](/wiki/Chinese_famine_of_1942%E2%80%931943 "Chinese famine of 1942–1943")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Engraving-FamineRelief-China.gif/250px-Engraving-FamineRelief-China.gif)](/wiki/File:Engraving-FamineRelief-China.gif)

[Chinese](/wiki/China "China") officials engaged in famine relief, 19th-century engraving

Chinese scholars had kept count of 1,828 instances of famine from 108 BCE to 1911 in one province or another—an average of more than one famine per year.[[67]](#cite_note-67) A major famine from 1333 to 1337 killed 6 million. The four famines of 1810, 1811, 1846, and 1849 are said to have killed no fewer than 45 million people.[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69)

China's [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") bureaucracy devoted extensive attention to minimizing famines with a network of [granaries](/wiki/Granaries "Granaries"). Its famines generally occurred immediately after [El Niño-Southern Oscillation](/wiki/El_Ni%C3%B1o-Southern_Oscillation "El Niño-Southern Oscillation")-linked droughts and floods. These events are comparable, though somewhat smaller in scale, to the ecological trigger events of China's vast 19th-century famines.[[70]](#cite_note-70) Qing China carried out its relief efforts, which included vast shipments of food, a requirement that the rich open their storehouses to the poor, and price regulation, as part of a state guarantee of subsistence to the peasantry (known as *ming-sheng*). However the [Taiping Rebellion](/wiki/Taiping_Rebellion "Taiping Rebellion") of the 1850s disrupted the granary relief system such that 1850 to 1873 saw the population of China drop by over 30 million people from early deaths and missing births.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

When a stressed monarchy shifted from state management and direct shipments of grain to monetary charity in the mid-19th century, the system broke down. Thus the 1867–68 famine under the [Tongzhi Restoration](/wiki/Tongzhi_Restoration "Tongzhi Restoration") was successfully relieved but the [Great North China Famine](/wiki/Great_North_China_Famine "Great North China Famine") of 1877–78, caused by drought across northern China, was a catastrophe. The province of [Shanxi](/wiki/Shanxi "Shanxi") was substantially depopulated as grains ran out, and desperately starving people stripped forests, fields, and their very houses for food. Estimated mortality is 9.5 to 13 million people.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

##### Great Leap Forward 1958–1961

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Great Leap Forward 1958–1961")]

The largest famine of the 20th century was [the 1958–1961 famine](/wiki/Great_Chinese_Famine "Great Chinese Famine") associated with the [Great Leap Forward](/wiki/Great_Leap_Forward "Great Leap Forward") in China. The immediate causes of this famine lay in Mao Zedong's ill-fated attempt to transform China from an agricultural nation to an industrial power in one huge leap. Communist Party cadres across China insisted that peasants abandon their farms for collective farms, and begin to produce steel in small foundries, often melting down their farm instruments in the process. Collectivisation undermined incentives for the investment of labor and resources in agriculture; unrealistic plans for decentralized metal production sapped needed labor; unfavorable weather conditions; and communal dining halls encouraged [overconsumption](/wiki/Overconsumption "Overconsumption") of available food.[[73]](#cite_note-73) Such was the centralized control of information and the intense pressure on party cadres to report only good news—such as [production quotas](/wiki/Production_quota "Production quota") met or exceeded—that information about the escalating disaster was effectively suppressed. When the leadership did become aware of the scale of the famine, it did little to respond, and continued to ban any discussion of the cataclysm. This blanket suppression of news was so effective that very few Chinese citizens were aware of the scale of the famine, and the greatest peacetime demographic disaster of the 20th century only became widely known twenty years later, when the veil of censorship began to lift.

The exact number of famine deaths during 1958–1961 is difficult to determine, and estimates range from 18 million[[74]](#cite_note-grada9-74) to at least 42 million[[75]](#cite_note-dikotterxii-75) people, with a further 30 million cancelled or delayed births.[[76]](#cite_note-76) It was only when the famine had wrought its worst that Mao reversed agricultural collectivisation policies, which were effectively dismantled in 1978. China has not experienced a famine of the proportions of the Great Leap Forward since 1961.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

#### Japan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Japan")]

[Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") experienced more than 130 famines between 1603 and 1868.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

#### Cambodia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Cambodia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Choeungek2.JPG/250px-Choeungek2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Choeungek2.JPG)

Skulls of [Khmer Rouge](/wiki/Khmer_Rouge "Khmer Rouge") murder victims at [Choeung Ek](/wiki/Choeung_Ek "Choeung Ek")

In 1975, the [Khmer Rouge](/wiki/Khmer_Rouge "Khmer Rouge") took control of Cambodia. The new government was led by [Pol Pot](/wiki/Pol_Pot "Pol Pot"), who desired to turn Cambodia into a communist, agrarian utopia. His regime emptied the cities, abolished currency and private property, and forced Cambodia's population into slavery on communal farms. In less than four years, the Khmer Rouge had executed nearly 1.4 million people, mostly those believed to be a threat to the new ideology.

Due to the failure of the Khmer Rouge's agrarian reform policies, Cambodia experienced widespread famine. As many as one million more died from starvation, disease, and exhaustion resulting from these policies.[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-kaplan-80) In 1979 Vietnam invaded Cambodia and removed the Khmer Rouge from power. By that time about one quarter of Cambodia's population had been killed.

#### North Korean famine in the 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: North Korean famine in the 1990s")]

[Famine struck North Korea in the mid-1990s](/wiki/North_Korean_famine "North Korean famine"), set off by unprecedented floods. This [autarkic](/wiki/Autarkic "Autarkic") [urban](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area"), industrial state depended on massive inputs of subsidised goods, including fossil fuels, primarily from the Soviet Union and the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China"). When the Soviet collapse and China's marketization switched trade to a hard currency, full-price basis, North Korea's economy collapsed. The vulnerable agricultural sector experienced a massive failure in 1995–96, expanding to full-fledged famine by 1996–1999.

Estimates based on the North Korean census suggest that 240,000 to 420,000 people died as a result of the famine and that there were 600,000 to 850,000 unnatural deaths in North Korea from 1993 to 2008.[[81]](#cite_note-81) North Korea has not yet regained food self-sufficiency and relies on external [food aid](/wiki/Food_aid "Food aid") from [China](/wiki/China "China"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). While Woo-Cumings have focused on the FAD side of the famine, Moon argues that FAD shifted the incentive structure of the authoritarian regime to react in a way that forced millions of disenfranchised people to starve to death.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

According to the [UN's](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") [Food and Agriculture Organization](/wiki/Food_and_Agriculture_Organization "Food and Agriculture Organization") (FAO), North Korea is facing a serious cereal shortfall in 2017 after the country's crop harvest was diminished as a result of severe drought.[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*][[83]](#cite_note-83) The FAO estimated that early-season production fell by over 30 percent compared to agricultural output from the previous year, leading to the country's worst famine since 2001.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

#### Vietnam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Vietnam")]

The [Japanese](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") occupation during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") caused the [Vietnamese famine of 1944–1945](/wiki/Vietnamese_famine_of_1944%E2%80%931945 "Vietnamese famine of 1944–1945"), which caused around 700,000 to 2,000,000 deaths.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: India")]

Main article: [Famine in India](/wiki/Famine_in_India "Famine in India")

See also: [Timeline of major famines in India during British rule](/wiki/Timeline_of_major_famines_in_India_during_British_rule "Timeline of major famines in India during British rule")

Owing to its almost entire dependence upon the [monsoon](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon") rains, India is vulnerable to crop failures, which upon occasion deepen into famine.[[86]](#cite_note-86) There were 14 famines in [India](/wiki/History_of_India "History of India") between the 11th and 17th centuries (Bhatia, 1985). For example, during the 1022–1033 Great famines in India entire provinces were depopulated. Famine in [Deccan](/wiki/Deccan "Deccan") killed at least two million people in 1702–1704. B.M. Bhatia believes that the earlier famines were localised, and it was only after 1860, during the [British rule](/wiki/British_rule "British rule"), that famine came to signify general shortage of foodgrains in the country. There were approximately 25 major famines spread through states such as [Tamil Nadu](/wiki/Tamil_Nadu "Tamil Nadu") in the south, and [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar") and [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal") in the east during the latter half of the 19th century.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/India-famine-family-crop-420.jpg/250px-India-famine-family-crop-420.jpg)](/wiki/File:India-famine-family-crop-420.jpg)

Victims of the Great Famine of 1876–78 in India during British rule, pictured in 1877.

[Romesh Chunder Dutt](/wiki/Romesh_Chunder_Dutt "Romesh Chunder Dutt") argued as early as 1900, and present-day scholars such as [Amartya Sen](/wiki/Amartya_Sen "Amartya Sen") agree, that some historic famines were a product of both uneven rainfall and British economic and administrative policies, which since 1857 had led to the seizure and conversion of local farmland to foreign-owned plantations, restrictions on internal trade, heavy taxation of Indian citizens to support British expeditions in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") (see [The Second Anglo-Afghan War](/wiki/European_influence_in_Afghanistan#Second_Anglo-Afghan_War "European influence in Afghanistan")), inflationary measures that increased the price of food, and substantial exports of staple crops from India to Britain. (Dutt, 1900 and 1902; Srivastava, 1968; Sen, 1982; Bhatia, 1985.)

Some British citizens, such as [William Digby](/wiki/William_Digby_(writer) "William Digby (writer)"), agitated for policy reforms and famine relief, but [Lord Lytton](/wiki/Robert_Bulwer-Lytton,_1st_Earl_of_Lytton "Robert Bulwer-Lytton, 1st Earl of Lytton"), the governing British viceroy in India, opposed such changes in the belief that they would stimulate shirking by Indian workers. The first, the [Bengal famine of 1770](/wiki/Bengal_famine_of_1770 "Bengal famine of 1770"), is estimated to have taken around 10 million lives—one-third of Bengal's population at the time. Other notable famines include the [Great Famine of 1876–1878](/wiki/Great_Famine_of_1876%E2%80%931878 "Great Famine of 1876–1878"), in which 6.1 million to 10.3 million people died[[87]](#cite_note-mikedavis7-87) and the [Indian famine of 1899–1900](/wiki/Indian_famine_of_1899%E2%80%931900 "Indian famine of 1899–1900"), in which 1.25 to 10 million people died.[[88]](#cite_note-mikedavis173-88) The famines were ended by the 20th century with the exception of the [Bengal famine of 1943](/wiki/Bengal_famine_of_1943 "Bengal famine of 1943") killing an estimated 2.1 million Bengalis during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[89]](#cite_note-89)

The observations of the Famine Commission of 1880 support the notion that food distribution is more to blame for famines than food scarcity. They observed that each province in [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India"), including [Burma](/wiki/British_Burma "British Burma"), had a surplus of foodgrains, and the annual surplus was 5.16 million tons (Bhatia, 1970). At that time, annual export of rice and other grains from India was approximately one million tons.

> Population growth worsened the plight of the peasantry. As a result of peace and improved sanitation and health, the Indian population rose from perhaps 100 million in 1700 to 300 million by 1920. While encouraging agricultural productivity, the British also provided economic incentives to have more children to help in the fields. Although a similar population increase occurred in Europe at the same time, the growing numbers could be absorbed by industrialization or emigration to the Americas and Australia. India enjoyed neither an industrial revolution nor an increase in food growing. Moreover, Indian landlords had a stake in the cash crop system and discouraged innovation. As a result, population numbers far outstripped the amount of available food and land, creating dire poverty and widespread hunger.

— Craig A. Lockard, *Societies, Networks, and Transitions*[[90]](#cite_note-90)

The Maharashtra drought saw zero deaths from starvation and is known for the successful employment of famine prevention policies, unlike during British rule.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

### Middle East

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Middle East")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Mother_and_child_during_the_Great_Famine_of_Mount_Lebanon_1.jpg/250px-Mother_and_child_during_the_Great_Famine_of_Mount_Lebanon_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mother_and_child_during_the_Great_Famine_of_Mount_Lebanon_1.jpg)

A starving woman and child during the [Great Famine of Mount Lebanon](/wiki/Great_Famine_of_Mount_Lebanon "Great Famine of Mount Lebanon"). Ottoman Empire, 1915-1918

The [Persian famine of 1870–1872](/wiki/Persian_famine_of_1870%E2%80%931872 "Persian famine of 1870–1872") is believed to have caused the death of 1.5 million persons (20–25% of the population) in [Persia](/wiki/Persia "Persia") (present-day Iran).[[92]](#cite_note-92)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/A_4-year-old_Palestinian_girl%2C_lost_her_life_due_to_malnutrition_and_lack_of_treatment_due_to_the_war_on_Gaza.jpg/250px-A_4-year-old_Palestinian_girl%2C_lost_her_life_due_to_malnutrition_and_lack_of_treatment_due_to_the_war_on_Gaza.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_4-year-old_Palestinian_girl,_lost_her_life_due_to_malnutrition_and_lack_of_treatment_due_to_the_war_on_Gaza.jpg)

Dead four year old Palestinian girl, 14 August 2024

In the early 20th century an Ottoman blockade of food being exported to [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon") caused a famine which killed up to 450,000 Lebanese (about one-third of the population). The famine killed more people than the [Lebanese Civil War](/wiki/Lebanese_Civil_War "Lebanese Civil War"). The blockade was caused by uprisings in the Syrian region of the Empire, including one which occurred in the 1860s which led to the massacre of thousands of Lebanese and Syrian by Ottoman Turks and local [Druze](/wiki/Druze "Druze").[[93]](#cite_note-93)

In 2025 Gaza has faced famine as a result of [Israeli airstrikes](/wiki/Israeli_airstrikes "Israeli airstrikes") during the [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war") and an [Israeli blockade](/wiki/Israeli_blockade_of_the_Gaza_Strip_(2023%E2%80%93present) "Israeli blockade of the Gaza Strip (2023–present)"), including of basic essentials and [humanitarian aid](/wiki/Humanitarian_aid_during_the_Gaza_war "Humanitarian aid during the Gaza war").[[94]](#cite_note-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-un20240318-96) Airstrikes have destroyed food infrastructure, such as bakeries, mills, and food stores, and there is a widespread scarcity of essential supplies due to the blockade of [aid](/wiki/Humanitarian_aid "Humanitarian aid"). According to a group of UN experts, as of July 2024 Israel's "targeted starvation campaign" had spread throughout the entire Gaza Strip, causing the death of [children](/wiki/Effect_of_the_Gaza_war_on_children_in_the_Gaza_Strip "Effect of the Gaza war on children in the Gaza Strip").[[97]](#cite_note-cnn20240709-97)[[98]](#cite_note-cbsnews20240709-98)[[99]](#cite_note-aljazeera20240709-99)[[100]](#cite_note-ohchr20240709-100) [Israel's mission to the UN](/wiki/Permanent_Representative_of_Israel_to_the_United_Nations "Permanent Representative of Israel to the United Nations") criticized the statement, calling it "misinformation".[[101]](#cite_note-timesofisrael20240709-101) The same month, detected cases of childhood malnutrition in northern Gaza increased by 300 percent compared to May 2024.[[102]](#cite_note-102) The UN-backed IPC confirmed famine is taking place in Gaza on 22 August 2025.[[103]](#cite_note-Nichols-103)

[Volker Türk](/wiki/Volker_T%C3%BCrk "Volker Türk"), the UN high commissioner for human rights, stated that [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel")'s restrictions on the entry of aid may constitute [starvation as a weapon of war](/wiki/Starvation_as_a_weapon_of_war "Starvation as a weapon of war"), which would be a [war crime](/wiki/War_crimes_in_the_Gaza_war "War crimes in the Gaza war").[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105) An [Independent International Commission of Inquiry](/wiki/Independent_International_Commission_of_Inquiry_on_the_Occupied_Palestinian_Territory "Independent International Commission of Inquiry on the Occupied Palestinian Territory") also found Israel was using starvation as a method of war.[[106]](#cite_note-106) In April and May, USAID and the US State Department's Bureau of Population, Refugees and Migration determined that Israel was blocking food aid from entering Gaza. These findings were rejected by Secretary of State Blinken and the Biden Administration.[[107]](#cite_note-107) The Israeli government has denied it is using starvation as a weapon of war and said that arguments that its actions regarding the famine violate the [Genocide Convention](/wiki/Genocide_Convention "Genocide Convention") are "wholly unfounded".[[108]](#cite_note-108) [COGAT](/wiki/Coordinator_of_Government_Activities_in_the_Territories "Coordinator of Government Activities in the Territories"), the Israeli agency responsible for allowing aid into Gaza, has stated Israel was not putting limits into the amount of aid entering Gaza.[[109]](#cite_note-109) COGAT's claim has been challenged by multiple entities, including the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), [Oxfam](/wiki/Oxfam "Oxfam"), and [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom").[[110]](#cite_note-110) Since March 2025, Israel has made the blockade publicly official,[[111]](#cite_note-111) with Defense Minister declaring that "No humanitarian aid will enter Gaza".[[112]](#cite_note-112) Israel has claimed that "Hamas stockpiled supplies and kept them from increasingly desperate civilians,"[[113]](#cite_note-DeYoung_Hudson_2024_g056-113)[[114]](#cite_note-114) but, as of February 2024, the US has not received evidence supporting this claim.[[115]](#cite_note-115) There have been reports of armed gangs stealing aid.[[116]](#cite_note-116)

### Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Europe")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Goya-Guerra_%2859%29.jpg/250px-Goya-Guerra_%2859%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Goya-Guerra_(59).jpg)

An engraving from [Goya](/wiki/Francisco_Goya "Francisco Goya")'s *[Disasters of War](/wiki/Disasters_of_War "Disasters of War")*, showing starving women, doubtless inspired by the terrible famine that struck [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid") in 1811–1812.

#### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

Further information: [Medieval demography](/wiki/Medieval_demography "Medieval demography"), [Crisis of the Late Middle Ages](/wiki/Crisis_of_the_Late_Middle_Ages "Crisis of the Late Middle Ages"), and [The General Crisis](/wiki/The_General_Crisis "The General Crisis")

The [Great Famine of 1315–1317](/wiki/Great_Famine_of_1315%E2%80%931317 "Great Famine of 1315–1317") (or to 1322) was the first major food crisis to strike Europe in the 14th century. Millions in northern Europe died over an extended number of years, marking a clear end to the earlier period of growth and prosperity during the 11th and 12th centuries.[[117]](#cite_note-117) An unusually cold and wet spring of 1315 led to widespread crop failures, which lasted until at least the summer of 1317; some regions in Europe did not fully recover until 1322. Most nobles, cities, and states were slow to respond to the crisis and when they realized its severity, they had little success in securing food for their people. In 1315, in [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk"), [England](/wiki/England "England"), the price of grain soared from 5 shillings/quarter to 20 shillings/quarter.[[118]](#cite_note-118) It was a period marked by extreme levels of criminal activity, disease and mass death, infanticide, and cannibalism. It had consequences for Church, State, European society and future calamities to follow in the 14th century. There were 95 famines in [medieval Britain](/wiki/Medieval_Britain "Medieval Britain"),[[119]](#cite_note-119) and 75 or more in medieval France.[[120]](#cite_note-120) More than 10% of England's population, or at least 500,000 people, may have died during the famine of 1315–1316.[[121]](#cite_note-famine-121)

Famine was a very destabilizing and devastating occurrence. The prospect of starvation led people to take desperate measures. When scarcity of food became apparent to peasants, they would sacrifice long-term prosperity for short-term survival. They would kill their [draught animals](/wiki/Draught_animal "Draught animal"), leading to lowered production in subsequent years. They would eat their seed corn, sacrificing next year's crop in the hope that more seed could be found. Once those means had been exhausted, they would take to the road in search of food. They migrated to the cities where merchants from other areas would be more likely to sell their food, as cities had a stronger purchasing power than did rural areas. Cities also administered relief programs and bought grain for their populations so that they could keep order. With the confusion and desperation of the migrants, crime would often follow them. Many peasants resorted to banditry in order to acquire enough to eat.

One famine would often lead to difficulties in the following years because of lack of seed stock or disruption of routine, or perhaps because of less-available labour. Famines were often interpreted as signs of God's displeasure. They were seen as the removal, by God, of His gifts to the people of the Earth. Elaborate religious processions and rituals were made to prevent God's wrath in the form of famine.

#### 16th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: 16th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Famine_in_Bretony%2C_c._1597.jpg/250px-Famine_in_Bretony%2C_c._1597.jpg)](/wiki/File:Famine_in_Bretony,_c._1597.jpg)

The 1590s saw a devastating famine in France exacerbated by the [French Wars of Religion](/wiki/French_Wars_of_Religion "French Wars of Religion").

During the 15th century to the 18th century, famines in Europe became more frequent due to the [Little Ice Age](/wiki/Little_Ice_Age "Little Ice Age"). The colder climate resulted in harvest failures and shortfalls that led to a rise in [conspiracy theories](/wiki/Conspiracy_theories "Conspiracy theories") concerning the causes behind these famines, such as the [Pacte de Famine](/wiki/Pacte_de_Famine "Pacte de Famine") in France.[[122]](#cite_note-122)

The 1590s saw the worst famines in centuries across all of Europe. Famine had been relatively rare during the 16th century. The economy and population had grown steadily as subsistence populations tend to when there is an extended period of relative peace (most of the time). Although peasants in areas of high population density, such as northern Italy, had learned to increase the yields of their lands through techniques such as promiscuous culture, they were still quite vulnerable to famines, forcing them to work their land even more intensively.

The great famine of the 1590s began a period of famine and decline in the 17th century. The price of [grain](/wiki/Grain "Grain"), all over Europe was high, as was the population. Various types of people were vulnerable to the succession of bad harvests that occurred throughout the 1590s in different regions. The increasing number of wage labourers in the countryside were vulnerable because they had no food of their own, and their meager living was not enough to purchase the expensive grain of a bad-crop year. Town labourers were also at risk because their wages would be insufficient to cover the cost of grain, and, to make matters worse, they often received less money in bad-crop years since the disposable income of the wealthy was spent on grain. Often, [unemployment](/wiki/Unemployment "Unemployment") would be the result of the increase in grain prices, leading to ever-increasing numbers of urban poor.

All areas of Europe were badly affected by the famine in these periods, especially rural areas. The Netherlands was able to escape most of the damaging effects of the famine, though the 1590s were still difficult years there. [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam")'s [grain trade](/wiki/Grain_trade "Grain trade") with the [Baltic](/wiki/Baltic_region "Baltic region") guaranteed a food supply.

#### 17th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: 17th century")]

The years around 1620 saw another period of famine sweep across Europe. These famines were generally less severe than the famines of twenty-five years earlier, but they were nonetheless quite serious in many areas. Perhaps the worst famine since 1600, the great famine in [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") in 1696, killed one-third of the population.[[123]](#cite_note-123)

Devastating harvest failures afflicted the northern Italian economy from 1618 to 1621, and it did not recover fully for centuries. There were serious famines in the late-1640s and less severe ones in the 1670s throughout northern Italy.

Over two million people died in two famines in France between 1693 and 1710. Both famines were made worse by ongoing wars.[[124]](#cite_note-124)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Starvation_image_from_F%C3%A4derneslandet_1867.jpg/250px-Starvation_image_from_F%C3%A4derneslandet_1867.jpg)](/wiki/File:Starvation_image_from_F%C3%A4derneslandet_1867.jpg)

Illustration of starvation in northern Sweden, [Swedish famine of 1867–1869](/wiki/Swedish_famine_of_1867%E2%80%931869 "Swedish famine of 1867–1869")

As late as the 1690s, Scotland experienced famine which reduced the population of parts of Scotland by at least 15%.[[125]](#cite_note-125)

The [Great Famine of 1695–1697](/wiki/Great_Famine_of_1695%E2%80%931697 "Great Famine of 1695–1697") may have killed a third of the Finnish population.[[126]](#cite_note-126) and roughly 10% of [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway")'s population.[[127]](#cite_note-127) Death rates rose in Scandinavia between 1740 and 1800 as the result of a series of crop failures.[[128]](#cite_note-128) For instance, the [Finnish famine of 1866–1868](/wiki/Finnish_famine_of_1866%E2%80%931868 "Finnish famine of 1866–1868") killed 15% of the population.

#### 18th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: 18th century")]

The period of 1740–1743 saw frigid winters and summer droughts, which led to famine across [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and a major spike in mortality.[[129]](#cite_note-129) The winter 1740–41 was unusually cold, possibly because of volcanic activity.[[130]](#cite_note-130)

According to Scott and Duncan (2002), "Eastern Europe experienced more than 150 recorded famines between AD 1500 and 1700 and there were 100 hunger years and 121 famine years in Russia between AD 971 and 1974."[[131]](#cite_note-131)

The [Great Famine](/wiki/Famines_in_Czech_lands "Famines in Czech lands"), which lasted from 1770 until 1771, killed about one tenth of [Czech lands](/wiki/Czech_lands "Czech lands")' population, or 250,000 inhabitants, and radicalised countrysides leading to peasant uprisings.[[132]](#cite_note-132)

There were sixteen good harvests and 111 famine years in northern Italy from 1451 to 1767.[[133]](#cite_note-133) According to Stephen L. Dyson and Robert J. Rowland, "The Jesuits of [Cagliari](/wiki/Cagliari "Cagliari") [in Sardinia] recorded years during the late 1500s 'of such hunger and so sterile that the majority of the people could sustain life only with wild ferns and other weeds' ... During the terrible famine of 1680, some 80,000 persons, out of a total population of 250,000, are said to have died, and entire villages were devastated".[[134]](#cite_note-134)

According to [Bryson](/wiki/Reid_Bryson "Reid Bryson") (1974), there were thirty-seven famine years in Iceland between 1500 and 1804.[[135]](#cite_note-135) In 1783 the volcano [Laki](/wiki/Laki "Laki") in south-central [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland") erupted. The lava caused little direct damage, but ash and sulphur dioxide spewed out over most of the country, causing three-quarters of the island's livestock to perish. In the following famine, around ten thousand people died, one-fifth of the population of [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland"). [Asimov, 1984, 152–53][*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

#### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Irish_potato_famine_Bridget_O%27Donnel.jpg/250px-Irish_potato_famine_Bridget_O%27Donnel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Irish_potato_famine_Bridget_O%27Donnel.jpg)

Depiction of victims of the [Great Famine](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)") in Ireland, 1845–1849

Other areas of Europe have known famines much more recently. France saw famines as recently as the 19th century. The [Great Famine](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)") in Ireland, 1846–1851, caused by the failure of the potato crop over a few years, resulted in 1,000,000 dead and another 2,000,000 refugees fleeing to Britain, Australia and the United States.[[136]](#cite_note-136)

#### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: 20th century")]

Famine still occurred in [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") during the 20th century. Droughts and famines in [Imperial Russia](/wiki/Imperial_Russia "Imperial Russia") are known to have happened every 10 to 13 years, with average droughts happening every 5 to 7 years. Russia experienced eleven major famines between 1845 and 1922, one of the worst being the [famine of 1891–1892](/wiki/Russian_famine_of_1891%E2%80%931892 "Russian famine of 1891–1892").[[137]](#cite_note-137) The [Russian famine of 1921–22](/wiki/Russian_famine_of_1921%E2%80%9322 "Russian famine of 1921–22") killed an estimated 5 million.
