[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/No-nb_bldsa_6a030.jpg/250px-No-nb_bldsa_6a030.jpg)](/wiki/File:No-nb_bldsa_6a030.jpg)

Victims of the [Russian famine of 1921–1922](/wiki/Russian_famine_of_1921%E2%80%931922 "Russian famine of 1921–1922") during the [Russian Civil War](/wiki/Russian_Civil_War "Russian Civil War")

[Famines continued](/wiki/Famines_in_Russia_and_USSR "Famines in Russia and USSR") in the [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet "Soviet") era, the most notorious being the *[Holodomor](/wiki/Holodomor "Holodomor")* in various parts of the country, especially the [Volga](/wiki/Volga_Region "Volga Region"), and the Ukrainian and northern [Kazakh](/wiki/Kazakhs "Kazakhs") SSR's during the winter of 1932–1933. The [Soviet famine of 1932–1933](/wiki/Soviet_famine_of_1932%E2%80%931933 "Soviet famine of 1932–1933") is nowadays reckoned to have cost an estimated 6 million lives.[[138]](#cite_note-138) The [last major famine](/wiki/Soviet_Famine_of_1947 "Soviet Famine of 1947") in the USSR happened in 1947 due to the severe [drought](/wiki/Drought "Drought") and the mismanagement of grain reserves by the Soviet government.[[139]](#cite_note-139)

The [Hunger Plan](/wiki/Hunger_Plan "Hunger Plan"), i.e. the Nazi plan to starve large sections of the Soviet population, caused the deaths of many. The Russian Academy of Sciences in 1995 reported civilian victims in the USSR at German hands, including Jews, totaled 13.7 million dead, 20% of the 68 million persons in the occupied USSR. This included 4.1 million famine and disease deaths in occupied territory. There were an additional estimated 3 million famine deaths in areas of the USSR not under German occupation.[[140]](#cite_note-140)

The 872 days of the [Siege of Leningrad](/wiki/Siege_of_Leningrad "Siege of Leningrad") (1941–1944) caused unparalleled famine in the Leningrad region through disruption of utilities, water, energy and food supplies. This resulted in the deaths of about one million people.[[141]](#cite_note-141)

Famine also struck in [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe") during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"). In the Netherlands, the *[Hongerwinter](/wiki/Hongerwinter "Hongerwinter")* of 1944 killed approximately 30,000 people. Some other areas of Europe also experienced famine at the same time.

### Latin America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Latin America")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/cc/Icon1_4_7.jpg)](/wiki/File:Icon1_4_7.jpg)

Malnourished child during Brazil's 1877–78 *Grande Seca* (Great Drought).

The [pre-Columbian](/wiki/Pre-Columbian "Pre-Columbian") Americans often dealt with severe food shortages and famines.[[142]](#cite_note-142) The persistent drought around 850 AD coincided with the collapse of [Classic Maya](/wiki/Classic_Maya_collapse "Classic Maya collapse") civilization, and the famine of One Rabbit (AD 1454) was a major catastrophe in [Mexico](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_Mexico "Pre-Columbian Mexico").[[143]](#cite_note-143)

[Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil")'s 1877–78 *[Grande Seca](/wiki/Grande_Seca "Grande Seca")* (Great Drought), the worst in Brazil's history,[[144]](#cite_note-144) caused approximately half a million deaths.[[145]](#cite_note-145) The one from 1915 was devastating too.[[146]](#cite_note-146)

### Oceania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Oceania")]

[Easter Island](/wiki/Easter_Island "Easter Island") was hit by a great famine between the 15th and 18th centuries. Hunger and subsequent cannibalism was caused by overpopulation and depletion of natural resources as a result of deforestation, partly because work on megalithic monuments required a lot of wood.[[147]](#cite_note-147)

There are other documented episodes of famine in various islands of Polynesia, such as occurred in [Kau, Hawaii](/wiki/Kau,_Hawaii "Kau, Hawaii") in 1868.[[148]](#cite_note-148)

According to Daniel Lord Smail, "'Famine [cannibalism](/wiki/Human_cannibalism "Human cannibalism")' was until recently a regular feature of life in the islands of the [Massim](/wiki/Massim "Massim") near [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea") and of some other societies of Southeast Asia and the Pacific."[[149]](#cite_note-149)

When Russian explorer [Otto von Kotzebue](/wiki/Otto_von_Kotzebue "Otto von Kotzebue") visited the [Marshall Islands](/wiki/Marshall_Islands "Marshall Islands") in Micronesia in 1817, he noted that Marshallese families practiced [infanticide](/wiki/Infanticide "Infanticide") after the birth of a third child as a form of [population planning](/wiki/Population_planning "Population planning") due to frequent famines.[[150]](#cite_note-150)

## Risk of future famine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Risk of future famine")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section's **[factual accuracy](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") may be compromised due to out-of-date information**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(December 2010)* |

[YALE climate connections](/wiki/YALE_Climate_Communications "YALE Climate Communications") reports that, as of 2022, approximately 34% of the world's agricultural land is degraded.[[151]](#cite_note-151) If current trends of soil degradation continue in Africa, the continent might be able to feed just 20% of its population by 2030, according to a [WHO](/wiki/WHO "WHO") study in 2024.[[152]](#cite_note-152) As of late 2007, increased farming for use in [biofuels](/wiki/Biofuel "Biofuel"),[[153]](#cite_note-153) along with world [oil prices](/wiki/Oil_prices "Oil prices") at nearly $100 a barrel,[[154]](#cite_note-154) has pushed up the price of grain used to feed poultry and dairy cows and other cattle, causing higher prices of wheat (up 58%), soybean (up 32%), and maize (up 11%) over the year.[[155]](#cite_note-155)[[156]](#cite_note-156) In 2007 [Food riots](/wiki/2007%E2%80%932008_world_food_price_crisis "2007–2008 world food price crisis") took place in many countries across the world.[[157]](#cite_note-157)[[158]](#cite_note-158)[[159]](#cite_note-159) An epidemic of stem rust, which is destructive to [wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat") and is caused by race [Ug99](/wiki/Ug99 "Ug99"), has in 2007 spread across Africa and into Asia.[[160]](#cite_note-160)[[161]](#cite_note-NewSci-161)

Beginning in the 20th century, nitrogen [fertilizers](/wiki/Fertilizer "Fertilizer"), new [pesticides](/wiki/Pesticide "Pesticide"), [desert farming](/wiki/Desert_farming "Desert farming"), and other agricultural technologies began to be used to increase food production, in part to combat famine. Between 1950 and 1984, as the [Green Revolution](/wiki/Green_Revolution "Green Revolution") influenced agriculture world grain production increased by 250%. Developed nations have shared these technologies with developing nations with a famine problem. However, as early as 1995, there were signs that these new developments may contribute to the decline of arable land (e.g. persistence of pesticides leading to [soil contamination](/wiki/Soil_contamination "Soil contamination"), [salt accumulation due to irrigation](/wiki/Soil_salinity "Soil salinity"), [erosion](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion")).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/ShrinkingLakeChad-1973-1997-EO.jpg/250px-ShrinkingLakeChad-1973-1997-EO.jpg)](/wiki/File:ShrinkingLakeChad-1973-1997-EO.jpg)

[Lake Chad](/wiki/Lake_Chad "Lake Chad") in a 2001 satellite image, with the actual lake in blue. The lake has shrunk by 95% since the 1960s.[[162]](#cite_note-162)

In 1994, David Pimentel, professor of ecology and [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture") at [Cornell University](/wiki/Cornell_University "Cornell University"), and Mario Giampietro, senior researcher at the National Research Institute on Food and Nutrition (INRAN), estimated the maximum [U.S. population](/wiki/U.S._population "U.S. population") for a [sustainable economy](/wiki/Sustainability "Sustainability") at 200 million.[[163]](#cite_note-163)

According to geologist [Dale Allen Pfeiffer](/wiki/Dale_Allen_Pfeiffer "Dale Allen Pfeiffer"), coming decades could see rising [food](/wiki/Food "Food") prices without relief and massive [starvation](/wiki/Starvation "Starvation") on a global level.[[164]](#cite_note-164) [Water deficits](/wiki/Water_deficits "Water deficits"), which are already spurring heavy [grain](/wiki/Grain "Grain") imports in numerous smaller countries, may soon do the same in larger countries, such as China or India.[[165]](#cite_note-165) The water tables are falling in many countries (including Northern China, the US, and India) due to widespread overconsumption. Other countries affected include Pakistan, Iran, and Mexico. This will eventually lead to [water scarcity](/wiki/Water_scarcity "Water scarcity") and cutbacks in grain harvest. Even while overexploiting its [aquifers](/wiki/Aquifers "Aquifers"), China has developed a grain deficit, contributing to the upward pressure on grain prices. Most of the three billion people projected to be added worldwide by mid-century will be born in countries already experiencing [water shortages](/wiki/Water_shortages "Water shortages").

After China and India, there is a second tier of smaller countries with large water deficits – Algeria, Egypt, Iran, Mexico, and Pakistan. Four of these already import a large share of their grain. Only Pakistan remains marginally self-sufficient. But with a population expanding by 4 million a year, it will also soon turn to the world market for grain.[[166]](#cite_note-166) According to a UN climate report, the [Himalayan](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas") glaciers that are the principal dry-season water sources of Asia's biggest rivers – [Ganges](/wiki/Ganges "Ganges"), [Indus](/wiki/Indus "Indus"), [Brahmaputra](/wiki/Brahmaputra "Brahmaputra"), [Yangtze](/wiki/Yangtze "Yangtze"), [Mekong](/wiki/Mekong "Mekong"), [Salween](/wiki/Salween "Salween") and [Yellow](/wiki/Yellow_River "Yellow River") – could disappear by 2350 as temperatures rise and human demand rises.[[note 1]](#cite_note-167)[[167]](#cite_note-168)[[168]](#cite_note-169) Approximately 2.4 billion people live in the [drainage basin](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin") of the Himalayan rivers.[[169]](#cite_note-170) India, China, Pakistan, Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Nepal and Myanmar could experience floods followed by severe droughts in coming decades.[[170]](#cite_note-171) In India alone, the Ganges provides water for drinking and farming for more than 500 million people.[[171]](#cite_note-172)[[172]](#cite_note-173)

Evan Fraser, a geographer at the [University of Guelph](/wiki/University_of_Guelph "University of Guelph") in [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"), Canada, explores the ways in which [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") may affect future famines.[[173]](#cite_note-174) To do this, he draws on a range of historic cases where relatively small environmental problems triggered famines as a way of creating theoretical links between climate and famine in the future. Drawing on situations as diverse as the [Great Famine of Ireland](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)"),[[174]](#cite_note-175) a series of weather induced famines in Asia during the late 19th century, and famines in Ethiopia during the 1980s, he concludes there are three "lines of defense" that protect a community's [food security](/wiki/Food_security "Food security") from [environmental change](/wiki/Environmental_change "Environmental change"). The first line of defense is the [agro-ecosystem](/wiki/Agroecosystem "Agroecosystem") on which food is produced: diverse ecosystems with well managed soils high in [organic matter](/wiki/Organic_matter "Organic matter") tend to be more resilient. The second line of defense is the wealth and skills of individual households: If those households affected by bad weather such as drought have savings or skills they may be able to do all right despite the bad weather.[[175]](#cite_note-176) The final line of defense is created by the formal institutions present in a society. Governments, churches, or NGOs must be willing and able to mount effective relief efforts. Pulling this together, Evan Fraser argues that if an [ecosystem](/wiki/Ecosystem "Ecosystem") is resilient enough, it may be able to withstand weather-related shocks. But if these shocks overwhelm the ecosystem's line of defense, it is necessary for the household to adapt using its skills and savings. If a problem is too big for the family or household, then people must rely on the third line of defense, which is whether or not the formal institutions present in a society are able to provide help. [Evan Fraser](/wiki/Evan_Fraser_(academic) "Evan Fraser (academic)") concludes that in almost every situation where an environmental problem triggered a famine you see a failure in each of these three lines of defense.[[176]](#cite_note-Fraser2006-177) Hence, understanding how climate change may cause famines in the future requires combining both an assessment of local socio-economic and environmental factors along with [climate models](/wiki/Climate_models "Climate models") that predict where bad weather may occur in the future.[[177]](#cite_note-178)[[178]](#cite_note-179)[[179]](#cite_note-180)

The [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), alongside lockdowns and travel restrictions, has prevented movement of aid and greatly impacted food production. As a result, several famines are forecast, which the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") called a crisis "of biblical proportions",[[180]](#cite_note-181) or "hunger pandemic".[[181]](#cite_note-182) This pandemic, in conjunction with the [2019-20 locust infestations](/wiki/2019-20_locust_infestation "2019-20 locust infestation") and several [ongoing armed conflicts](/wiki/Ongoing_armed_conflicts "Ongoing armed conflicts"), is predicted to form the worst series of famines since the [Great Chinese Famine](/wiki/Great_Chinese_Famine "Great Chinese Famine"), affecting between 10 and 20 percent of the global population in some way.[[182]](#cite_note-183)[[183]](#cite_note-184)

Western nations suspended humanitarian aid to [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") following the [Taliban](/wiki/Taliban "Taliban")'s takeover of the country in August 2021.[[184]](#cite_note-185) The United States has [frozen about $9 billion](/wiki/Afghan_frozen_assets "Afghan frozen assets") in assets belonging to the [Afghan central banks](/wiki/Da_Afghanistan_Bank "Da Afghanistan Bank"),[[185]](#cite_note-186) blocking the Taliban from accessing billions of dollars held in U.S. bank accounts.[[186]](#cite_note-187)[[187]](#cite_note-188) In October 2021, more than half of Afghanistan's 39 million people faced an acute [food shortage](/wiki/Food_security "Food security").[[188]](#cite_note-189) On 11 November 2021, the *[Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch")* reported that Afghanistan is facing widespread famine due to collapsed economy and broken banking system. The [UN World Food Programme](/wiki/UN_World_Food_Programme "UN World Food Programme") has also issued multiple warnings of worsening food insecurity.[[189]](#cite_note-190)

## Causes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Causes")]

See also: [Theories of famines](/wiki/Theories_of_famines "Theories of famines")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/%D0%94%D0%B8%D1%81%D1%82%D1%80%D0%BE%D1%84%D0%B8%D1%8F_%D0%B0%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BC%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%82%D0%B0%D1%80%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%8F.jpg/250px-%D0%94%D0%B8%D1%81%D1%82%D1%80%D0%BE%D1%84%D0%B8%D1%8F_%D0%B0%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BC%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%82%D0%B0%D1%80%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%8F.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%94%D0%B8%D1%81%D1%82%D1%80%D0%BE%D1%84%D0%B8%D1%8F_%D0%B0%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BC%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%82%D0%B0%D1%80%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%8F.jpg)

A victim of starvation in [besieged Leningrad](/wiki/Siege_of_Leningrad "Siege of Leningrad") suffering from [dystrophia](/wiki/Dystrophia "Dystrophia") in 1941.[[190]](#cite_note-191)

Definitions of famines are based on three different categories—these include food supply-based, food consumption-based and mortality-based definitions. Some definitions of famines are:

* Blix – Widespread food shortage leading to significant rise in regional death rates.[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlixSvensk_näringsforskning1971-192)
* Brown and Eckholm – Sudden, sharp reduction in food supply resulting in widespread hunger.[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownEckholm1974-193)
* Scrimshaw – Sudden collapse in level of food consumption of large numbers of people.[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScrimshaw1987-194)
* Ravallion – Unusually high mortality with unusually severe threat to food intake of some segments of a population.[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERavallion19962-195)
* Cuny – A set of conditions that occurs when large numbers of people in a region cannot obtain sufficient food, resulting in widespread, acute malnutrition.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECunyHill1999-196)

Food shortages in a population are caused either by a lack of food or by difficulties in food distribution; it may be worsened by natural climate fluctuations and by extreme political conditions related to oppressive government or warfare. The conventional explanation until 1981 for the cause of famines was the Food availability decline (FAD) hypothesis. The assumption was that the central cause of all famines was a decline in food availability.[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEncyclopædia_Britannica2010-197) However, FAD could not explain why only a certain section of the population such as the agricultural laborer was affected by famines while others were insulated from famines.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChaudhari1984135-198) Based on the studies of some recent famines, the decisive role of FAD has been questioned and it has been suggested that the causal mechanism for precipitating starvation includes many variables other than just decline of food availability. According to this view, famines are a result of entitlements, the theory being proposed is called the "failure of exchange entitlements" or FEE.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChaudhari1984135-198) A person may own various commodities that can be exchanged in a market economy for the other commodities he or she needs. The exchange can happen via trading or production or through a combination of the two. These entitlements are called trade-based or production-based entitlements. Per this proposed view, famines are precipitated due to a breakdown in the ability of the person to exchange his entitlements.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChaudhari1984135-198) An example of famines due to FEE is the inability of an agricultural laborer to exchange his primary entitlement, i.e., labor for rice when his employment became erratic or was eliminated.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChaudhari1984135-198)

According to the Physicians for Social Responsibility (PSR), global [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") is additionally challenging the Earth's ability to produce food, potentially leading to famine.[[198]](#cite_note-Psrccaf-199)

Some elements make a particular region more vulnerable to famine. These include poverty, [population growth](/wiki/Population_growth "Population growth"),[[199]](#cite_note-200) an inappropriate social infrastructure, a suppressive political regime, and a weak or under-prepared government.[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERavallion19961-201)

According to [Oxfam International](/wiki/Oxfam_International "Oxfam International"), commenting on a [Famine Early Warning Systems Network](/wiki/Famine_Early_Warning_Systems_Network "Famine Early Warning Systems Network") (FEWS NET) report, "Famines are not natural phenomena, they are catastrophic political failures."[[201]](#cite_note-202)

### Climate and population pressure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Climate and population pressure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Starved_child.jpg/250px-Starved_child.jpg)](/wiki/File:Starved_child.jpg)

A child suffering extreme starvation in India, 1972

Thomas Malthus's *Essay on the Principle of Population* has made popular the theory of the [Malthusian catastrophe](/wiki/Malthusian_catastrophe "Malthusian catastrophe")—that many famines are caused by imbalance of food production compared to the large populations of countries[[202]](#cite_note-de_Waal_2018-203) whose [population exceeds the regional carrying capacity](/wiki/Human_overpopulation "Human overpopulation").[[203]](#cite_note-204) However, anthropologist [Alex de Waal](/wiki/Alex_de_Waal "Alex de Waal"), executive director of the World Peace Foundation,[[204]](#cite_note-205) refutes the Malthus theory, looking instead to political factors as major causes of recent (over the last 150 years) famines.[[202]](#cite_note-de_Waal_2018-203) Historically, famines have occurred from agricultural problems such as drought, crop failure, or [pestilence](/wiki/Infectious_disease "Infectious disease"). Changing weather patterns, the ineffectiveness of medieval governments in dealing with crises, wars, and [epidemic diseases](/wiki/List_of_epidemics "List of epidemics") such as the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death") helped to cause hundreds of famines in Europe during the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), including 95 in Britain and 75 in France.[[205]](#cite_note-206) In France, the [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War"), crop failures and epidemics reduced the population by two-thirds.[[206]](#cite_note-207) Economist [Amartya Sen](/wiki/Amartya_Sen "Amartya Sen") has also argued that sustained famines historically have been caused by political instability and repressive political regimes rather than overpopulation.[[207]](#cite_note-208)[[208]](#cite_note-209)

The failure of a harvest or change in conditions, such as [drought](/wiki/Drought "Drought"), can create a situation whereby large numbers of people continue to live where the [carrying capacity](/wiki/Carrying_capacity "Carrying capacity") of the land has temporarily dropped radically. Famine is often associated with [subsistence](/wiki/Subsistence "Subsistence") agriculture. The total absence of agriculture in an economically strong area does not cause famine; Arizona and other wealthy regions import the vast majority of their food, since such regions produce sufficient economic goods for trade.

Famines have also been caused by volcanism. The 1815 eruption of the [Mount Tambora](/wiki/Mount_Tambora "Mount Tambora") volcano in Indonesia caused crop failures and famines worldwide and caused the worst famine of the 19th century. The current consensus of the scientific community is that the aerosols and dust released into the upper atmosphere causes cooler temperatures by preventing the sun's energy from reaching the ground. The same mechanism is theorized to be caused by very large meteorite impacts to the extent of causing mass extinctions.

### State-sponsored famines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: State-sponsored famines")]

See also: [Starvation (crime)](/wiki/Starvation_(crime) "Starvation (crime)")

In certain cases, such as the [Great Leap Forward](/wiki/Great_Leap_Forward "Great Leap Forward") in China (which produced the [largest famine in absolute numbers](/wiki/Great_Chinese_Famine "Great Chinese Famine")), [Francoist Spain](/wiki/Francoist_Spain "Francoist Spain")'s *years of hunger*,[[209]](#cite_note-sage_franco-210)[[210]](#cite_note-radboud_franco-211)[[211]](#cite_note-roman_franco-212)[[212]](#cite_note-santiago_franco-213)[[213]](#cite_note-delarco_franco-214) [North Korea in the mid-1990s](/wiki/North_Korean_famine "North Korean famine"), or [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe") in the early-2000s, famine can occur because of government policy.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/5/5e/HolodomorKharkiv.jpg/250px-HolodomorKharkiv.jpg)](/wiki/File:HolodomorKharkiv.jpg)

The government's [forced collectivization](/wiki/Collectivization_in_the_Soviet_Union "Collectivization in the Soviet Union") of agriculture was one of the main causes of the [Soviet famine of 1932–1933](/wiki/Soviet_famine_of_1932%E2%80%931933 "Soviet famine of 1932–1933").

According to [Simon Payaslian](/wiki/Simon_Payaslian "Simon Payaslian"), a tentative scholarly consensus classifies the Soviet famine (at least in Ukraine where 2.5 to 4 million perished[[214]](#cite_note-215)) as a [genocide](/wiki/Genocide "Genocide"),[[215]](#cite_note-216) although some scholars say that it remains a significant issue in modern politics and dispute whether Soviet policies would fall under the [legal definition of genocide](/wiki/Legal_definition_of_genocide "Legal definition of genocide").[[216]](#cite_note-217)[[217]](#cite_note-218) Several scholars have disputed that the famine was a [genocidal act](/wiki/Genocide_by_attrition "Genocide by attrition") by the Soviet government, including [J. Arch Getty](/wiki/J._Arch_Getty "J. Arch Getty"),[[218]](#cite_note-Getty_2000-219) [Stephen G. Wheatcroft](/wiki/Stephen_G._Wheatcroft "Stephen G. Wheatcroft"),[[219]](#cite_note-Wheatcroft_2018-220) [R. W. Davies](/wiki/R._W._Davies "R. W. Davies"),[[220]](#cite_note-221) and Mark Tauger.[[221]](#cite_note-222) Getty says that the "overwhelming weight of opinion among scholars working in the new archives ... is that the terrible famine of the 1930s was the result of Stalinist bungling and rigidity rather than some genocidal plan."[[218]](#cite_note-Getty_2000-219) Wheatcroft says that the Soviet government's policies during the famine were criminal acts of fraud and manslaughter, though not outright murder or genocide.[[222]](#cite_note-Wheatcroft_2020-223)[[note 2]](#cite_note-224) In regard to the Soviet state's reaction to this crisis, Wheatcroft comments: "The good harvest of 1930 led to the decisions to export substantial amounts of grain in 1931 and 1932. The Soviet leaders also assumed that the wholesale socialisation of livestock farming would lead to the rapid growth of meat and dairy production. These policies failed, and the Soviet leaders attributed the failure not to their own lack of realism but to the machinations of enemies. Peasant resistance was blamed on the kulaks, and the increased use of force on a large scale almost completely replaced attempts at persuasion."[[223]](#cite_note-Davies_&_Wheatcroft_2009-225) Wheatcroft says that Soviet authorities refused to scale down grain procurements despite the low harvest,[[219]](#cite_note-Wheatcroft_2018-220) and that "[Wheatcroft and his colleague's] work has confirmed – if confirmation were needed – that the grain campaign in 1932/33 was unprecedentedly harsh and repressive."[[224]](#cite_note-Davies_&_Wheatcroft_2004-226)

[Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin") biographer [Stephen Kotkin](/wiki/Stephen_Kotkin "Stephen Kotkin") supports a similar view, stating that while "there is no question of Stalin's responsibility for the famine" and many deaths could have been prevented if not for the "insufficient" and counterproductive Soviet measures, there is no evidence for Stalin's intention to kill the Ukrainians deliberately.[[225]](#cite_note-Kotkin_2017-227) While Mark Tauger considers the famine to be the result of natural factors stating that "the harsh 1932–1933 procurements only displaced the famine from urban areas" but the low harvest "made a famine inevitable". Ultimately concluding that it is difficult to accept the famine "as the result of the 1932 grain procurements and as a conscious act of [genocide](/wiki/Genocide "Genocide")" he still concurs with Wheatcroft that "the regime was still responsible for the deprivation and suffering of the Soviet population in the early 1930s", and "if anything, these data show that the effects of [collectivization and forced industrialization] were worse than has been assumed."[[226]](#cite_note-228)

In 1958 in China, [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong")'s Communist Government launched the [Great Leap Forward](/wiki/Great_Leap_Forward "Great Leap Forward") campaign, aimed at rapidly industrializing the country.[[227]](#cite_note-NewYorkTimes-229) The government forcibly took control of agriculture. Barely enough grain was left for the peasants, and starvation occurred in many rural areas. Exportation of grain continued despite the famine and the government attempted to conceal it. While the famine is attributed to unintended consequences, it is believed that the government refused to acknowledge the problem, thereby further contributing to the deaths. In many instances, peasants were persecuted. Between 20 and 45 million people perished in this famine, making it one of the deadliest famines to date.[[228]](#cite_note-230)

Historian and journalists, such as [Seumas Milne](/wiki/Seumas_Milne "Seumas Milne") and [Jon Wiener](/wiki/Jon_Wiener "Jon Wiener"), have criticized the emphasis on [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism") when assigning blame for famines. In a 2002 article for *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*, Milne mentions "the moral blindness displayed towards the record of [colonialism](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism")", and he writes: "If Lenin and Stalin are regarded as having killed those who died of hunger in the famines of the 1920s and 1930s, then Churchill is certainly responsible for the 4 million deaths in the avoidable [Bengal famine of 1943](/wiki/Bengal_famine_of_1943 "Bengal famine of 1943").[[229]](#cite_note-231) Weiner makes a similar assertion while comparing the [Holodomor](/wiki/Holodomor "Holodomor") and the Bengal famine of 1943, stating that [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill")'s role in the Bengal famine "seems similar to Stalin's role in the Ukrainian famine".[[230]](#cite_note-232) Historian [Mike Davis](/wiki/Mike_Davis_(scholar) "Mike Davis (scholar)"), author of *[Late Victorian Holocausts](/wiki/Late_Victorian_Holocausts "Late Victorian Holocausts")*, draws comparisons between the [Great Chinese Famine](/wiki/Great_Chinese_Famine "Great Chinese Famine") and the [Indian famines of the late 19th century](/wiki/Timeline_of_major_famines_in_India_during_British_rule "Timeline of major famines in India during British rule"), arguing that in both instances the governments which oversaw the response to the famines deliberately chose not to alleviate conditions and as such bear responsibility for the scale of deaths in said famines.[[231]](#cite_note-233) According to [Jason Hickel](/wiki/Jason_Hickel "Jason Hickel") and Dylan Sullivan, the number excess deaths during the apex of British colonialism in India rise to around 100 million.[[232]](#cite_note-234)[[233]](#cite_note-235)

Malawi ended its famine by subsidizing farmers despite the strictures imposed by the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank").[[234]](#cite_note-newyorktimes-236) During the 1973 [Wollo Famine](/wiki/Haile_Selassie_I_of_Ethiopia#Wollo_famine "Haile Selassie I of Ethiopia") in [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia"), food was shipped out of Wollo to the capital city of [Addis Ababa](/wiki/Addis_Ababa "Addis Ababa"), where it could command higher prices. In the late-1970s and early-1980s, residents of the [dictatorships](/wiki/Dictatorship "Dictatorship") of Ethiopia and [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan") suffered massive famines, but the [democracy](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") of [Botswana](/wiki/Botswana "Botswana") avoided them, despite also suffering a severe drop in national food production. In [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia"), famine occurred because of a [failed state](/wiki/Failed_state "Failed state").

The [famine in Yemen](/wiki/Famine_in_Yemen "Famine in Yemen") is a direct result of the [Saudi Arabian-led intervention in Yemen](/wiki/Saudi_Arabian-led_intervention_in_Yemen "Saudi Arabian-led intervention in Yemen") and the [blockade](/wiki/Blockade_of_Yemen "Blockade of Yemen") imposed by Saudi Arabia and its allies, including the United States.[[235]](#cite_note-237)[[236]](#cite_note-238) According to the UN, 130 children under five years of age were dying from starvation and starvation related diseases every day by the end of 2017, with 50,000 dead for the year. As of October 2018, half the population is at risk of famine.[[237]](#cite_note-239)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Famines_by_political_regime.png/250px-Famines_by_political_regime.png)](/wiki/File:Famines_by_political_regime.png)

Famines since 1850 by political regime. Large catastrophic famines have occurred predominantly in autocratic regimes.

Israel's [blockade of Gaza](/wiki/2023_Israeli_blockade_of_the_Gaza_Strip "2023 Israeli blockade of the Gaza Strip") and the [war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war") between Israel and Hamas led to a [famine in the Gaza Strip](/wiki/Famine_in_the_Gaza_Strip "Famine in the Gaza Strip") in 2024.[[238]](#cite_note-240) On 16 January 2024, UN experts accused Israel of "destroying Gaza's food system and using food as a weapon against the Palestinian people".[[239]](#cite_note-241) The UN-backed IPC confirmed famine is taking place in Gaza on 22 August 2025.[[103]](#cite_note-Nichols-103)

According to Amartya Sen (1999), "there has never been a famine in a functioning multiparty democracy". Hasell and Roser have demonstrated that while there have been a few minor exceptions, famines rarely occur in democratic systems but are strongly correlated with [autocratic](/wiki/Autocratic "Autocratic") and [colonial](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism") systems.[[240]](#cite_note-242)

### Economic sanctions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Economic sanctions")]

[Economic sanctions](/wiki/Economic_sanctions "Economic sanctions") are state or UN sanctioned measures to pressure a targeted country to submit to political demands. Because such measures often involve limiting imports and exports to "crash" or inflict pain on the targeted nation's economy. This can restrict the targeted nation's economic development and make it more difficult for their population to access food.[[241]](#cite_note-243)[[242]](#cite_note-244) Economic sanctions have been blamed for contributing to the [North Korean famine](/wiki/North_Korean_famine "North Korean famine") in the 1990s.[[243]](#cite_note-245) The effects of [sanctions against Iraq](/wiki/Sanctions_against_Iraq "Sanctions against Iraq") in the 1990s on mortality are disputed.[[244]](#cite_note-246) Following the [Taliban](/wiki/Taliban "Taliban")'s takeover of Afghanistan in August 2021, [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch") warned that Afghanistan was at risk of famine and urged the UN, World Bank, US and other countries to modify their sanctions to help prevent famine.[[245]](#cite_note-247)

## Famine prevention

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Famine prevention")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Famine_in_Algeria_1869.jpg/250px-Famine_in_Algeria_1869.jpg)](/wiki/File:Famine_in_Algeria_1869.jpg)

A starving child during the 1869 famine in [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria").

Relief technologies, including [immunization](/wiki/Immunization "Immunization"), improved [public health](/wiki/Public_health "Public health") infrastructure, general food rations and supplementary feeding for vulnerable children, has provided temporary mitigation to the mortality impact of famines, while leaving their economic consequences unchanged, and not solving the underlying issue of too large a regional population relative to food production capability. Humanitarian crises may also arise from [genocide](/wiki/Genocide "Genocide") campaigns, [civil wars](/wiki/Civil_war "Civil war"), [agro-terrorism](/wiki/Agro-terrorism "Agro-terrorism"), [refugee](/wiki/Refugee "Refugee") flows and episodes of extreme violence and state collapse, creating famine conditions among the affected populations.[[246]](#cite_note-248)

Despite repeated stated intentions by the world's leaders to end hunger and famine, famine remains a chronic threat in much of Africa, Eastern Europe, the Southeast, South Asia, and the Middle East. In July 2005, the [Famine Early Warning Systems Network](/wiki/Famine_Early_Warning_Systems_Network "Famine Early Warning Systems Network") (FEWS NET) labelled Niger with emergency status, as well as Chad, Ethiopia, [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan"), Somalia and Zimbabwe. In January 2006, the United Nations [Food and Agriculture Organization](/wiki/Food_and_Agriculture_Organization "Food and Agriculture Organization") warned that 11 million people in Somalia, [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya"), [Djibouti](/wiki/Djibouti "Djibouti") and Ethiopia were in danger of starvation due to the combination of severe drought and military conflicts.[[247]](#cite_note-249) In 2006, the most serious humanitarian crisis in Africa was in [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan")'s region [Darfur](/wiki/Darfur "Darfur").[[248]](#cite_note-250)

[Frances Moore Lappé](/wiki/Frances_Moore_Lapp%C3%A9 "Frances Moore Lappé"), later co-founder of the [Institute for Food and Development Policy](/wiki/Institute_for_Food_and_Development_Policy "Institute for Food and Development Policy") (Food First) argued in *[Diet for a Small Planet](/wiki/Diet_for_a_Small_Planet "Diet for a Small Planet")* (1971) that vegetarian diets can provide food for larger populations, with the same resources, compared to omnivorous diets.

Noting that modern famines are sometimes aggravated by misguided economic policies, political design to impoverish or marginalize certain populations, or acts of war, political economists have investigated the political conditions under which famine is prevented. Economist [Amartya Sen](/wiki/Amartya_Sen "Amartya Sen")[[note 3]](#cite_note-252) states that the liberal institutions that exist in India, including competitive elections and a free press, have played a major role in preventing famine in that country since independence. [Alex de Waal](/wiki/Alex_de_Waal "Alex de Waal") has developed this theory to focus on the "political contract" between rulers and people that ensures famine prevention, noting the rarity of such political contracts in Africa, and the danger that international relief agencies will undermine such contracts through removing the locus of accountability for famines from national governments.

The demographic impacts of famine are sharp. Mortality is concentrated among children and the elderly. A consistent demographic fact is that in all recorded famines, male mortality exceeds female, even in those populations (such as northern India and Pakistan) where there is a male longevity advantage during normal times. Reasons for this may include greater female resilience under the pressure of [malnutrition](/wiki/Malnutrition "Malnutrition"), and possibly female's naturally higher percentage of body fat. Famine is also accompanied by lower fertility. Famines therefore leave the reproductive core of a population—adult women—lesser affected compared to other population categories, and post-famine periods are often characterized a "rebound" with increased births.

Even though the theories of [Thomas Malthus](/wiki/Thomas_Malthus "Thomas Malthus") would predict that famines reduce the size of the population commensurate with available food resources, in fact even the most severe famines have rarely dented population growth for more than a few years. The mortality in China in 1958–61, Bengal in 1943, and Ethiopia in 1983–85 was all made up by a growing population over just a few years. Of greater long-term demographic impact is emigration: Ireland was chiefly depopulated after the [1840s famines](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)") by waves of emigration.

### Overall food production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Overall food production")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Food_production_per_capita.svg/250px-Food_production_per_capita.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Food_production_per_capita.svg)

The [Green Revolution](/wiki/Green_Revolution "Green Revolution") significantly increased food production, enabling it to outpace population growth and avert widespread famine, particularly in the latter half of the 20th century.

Globally, the amount of food produced per person has kept rising, despite a growing world population. A local crop failure does not cause a famine unless there is also a lack of money to buy food from elsewhere. A war or political oppression can also disrupt distribution of otherwise adequate global supplies.[[250]](#cite_note-253)

### Food security

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Food security")]

Main article: [Food security](/wiki/Food_security "Food security")

Long term measures to improve food security, include investment in modern agriculture techniques, such as [fertilizers](/wiki/Fertilizers "Fertilizers") and [irrigation](/wiki/Irrigation "Irrigation"),[[251]](#cite_note-obama-254) but can also include strategic national [food storage](/wiki/Food_storage "Food storage").

[World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") strictures restrict government subsidies for farmers, and increasing use of fertilizers is opposed by some environmental groups because of its unintended consequences: adverse effects on water supplies and habitat.[[234]](#cite_note-newyorktimes-236)[[252]](#cite_note-atlantic-255)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Norman_Borlaug.jpg/250px-Norman_Borlaug.jpg)](/wiki/File:Norman_Borlaug.jpg)

[Norman Borlaug](/wiki/Norman_Borlaug "Norman Borlaug"), father of the Green Revolution, is often credited with saving over a billion people worldwide from starvation.

The effort to bring modern agricultural techniques found in the [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world"), such as [nitrogen](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen") [fertilizers](/wiki/Fertilizers "Fertilizers") and [pesticides](/wiki/Pesticides "Pesticides"), to the [Indian Sub-continent](/wiki/Indian_Sub-continent "Indian Sub-continent"), called the [Green Revolution](/wiki/Green_Revolution "Green Revolution"), resulted in decreases in malnutrition similar to those seen earlier in Western nations. This was possible because of existing [infrastructure](/wiki/Infrastructure "Infrastructure") and institutions that are in short supply in [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), such as a system of roads or public [seed](/wiki/Seed "Seed") companies that made seeds available.[[253]](#cite_note-256) Supporting farmers in areas of food insecurity, through such measures as free or subsidized [fertilizers](/wiki/Fertilizer "Fertilizer") and [seeds](/wiki/Seed "Seed"), increases food harvest and reduces food prices.[[234]](#cite_note-newyorktimes-236)[[254]](#cite_note-257)

The energy for the Green Revolution was provided by [fossil fuels](/wiki/Fossil_fuels "Fossil fuels") in the form of fertilizers (natural gas), pesticides (oil), and [hydrocarbon](/wiki/Hydrocarbon "Hydrocarbon") fueled [irrigation](/wiki/Irrigation "Irrigation").[[255]](#cite_note-258)[[256]](#cite_note-259) The development of synthetic nitrogen fertilizer has significantly supported global [population growth](/wiki/Population_growth "Population growth") — it has been estimated that almost half the people on the Earth are currently fed as a result of synthetic nitrogen fertilizer use.[[257]](#cite_note-260)[[258]](#cite_note-261)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/World_population_supported_by_synthetic_nitrogen_fertilizers%2C_OWID.svg/250px-World_population_supported_by_synthetic_nitrogen_fertilizers%2C_OWID.svg.png)](/wiki/File:World_population_supported_by_synthetic_nitrogen_fertilizers,_OWID.svg)

[World population](/wiki/World_population "World population") supported with and without synthetic nitrogen [fertilizers](/wiki/Fertilizer "Fertilizer").

The [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") and some rich nations press nations that depend on them for aid to cut back or eliminate subsidized agricultural inputs such as fertilizer, in the name of [privatization](/wiki/Privatization "Privatization") even as the United States and Europe extensively subsidized their own farmers.[[259]](#cite_note-262)

### Relief

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Relief")]

Main article: [Famine relief](/wiki/Famine_relief "Famine relief")

There is a growing realization among aid groups that giving cash or cash vouchers instead of food is a cheaper, faster, and more efficient way to deliver help to the hungry, particularly in areas where food is available but unaffordable.[[260]](#cite_note-csmonitor-263) The United Nations [World Food Programme](/wiki/World_Food_Programme "World Food Programme"), the biggest non-governmental distributor of food, announced that it will begin distributing cash and vouchers instead of food in some areas, which [Josette Sheeran](/wiki/Josette_Sheeran "Josette Sheeran"), WFP's former executive director, described as a "[revolution](/wiki/Revolution "Revolution")" in food aid.[[260]](#cite_note-csmonitor-263)[[261]](#cite_note-wfp-264) The aid agency [Concern Worldwide](/wiki/Concern_Worldwide "Concern Worldwide") is piloting a method through a mobile phone operator, Safaricom, which runs a money transfer program that allows cash to be sent from one part of the country to another.[[260]](#cite_note-csmonitor-263)

However, for people in a [drought](/wiki/Drought "Drought") living a long way from and with limited access to [markets](/wiki/Marketplace "Marketplace"), delivering food may be the most appropriate way to help.[[260]](#cite_note-csmonitor-263) [Fred Cuny](/wiki/Fred_Cuny "Fred Cuny") stated that "the chances of saving lives at the outset of a relief operation are greatly reduced when food is imported. By the time it arrives in the country and gets to people, many will have died."[[262]](#cite_note-265) US Law[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*], which requires buying food at home rather than where the hungry live, is inefficient because approximately half of what is spent goes for transport.[[263]](#cite_note-266) Fred Cuny further pointed out "studies of every recent famine have shown that food was available in-country—though not always in the immediate food deficit area" and "even though by local standards the prices are too high for the poor to purchase it, it would usually be cheaper for a donor to buy the hoarded food at the inflated price than to import it from abroad."[[264]](#cite_note-267)

Deficient [micronutrients](/wiki/Micronutrient "Micronutrient") can be provided through [fortifying](/wiki/Food_fortification "Food fortification") foods.[[265]](#cite_note-hiddenhunger-268) Fortifying foods such as [peanut butter](/wiki/Peanut_butter "Peanut butter") sachets (see [Plumpy'Nut](/wiki/Plumpy%27Nut "Plumpy'Nut")) have revolutionized emergency feeding in humanitarian emergencies because they can be eaten directly from the packet, do not require refrigeration or mixing with scarce clean water, can be stored for years and, vitally, can be absorbed by extremely ill children.[[266]](#cite_note-BBC-269)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/VOA_Heinlein_-_Somali_refugees_September_2011_-_09.jpg/250px-VOA_Heinlein_-_Somali_refugees_September_2011_-_09.jpg)](/wiki/File:VOA_Heinlein_-_Somali_refugees_September_2011_-_09.jpg)

A [Somali](/wiki/Somali_people "Somali people") boy receiving treatment for malnutrition at a health facility in Hilaweyn during the [drought of 2011](/wiki/2011_East_Africa_drought "2011 East Africa drought").

[WHO](/wiki/WHO "WHO") and other sources recommend that malnourished children—and adults who also have [diarrhea](/wiki/Diarrhea "Diarrhea")—drink rehydration solution, and continue to eat, in addition to antibiotics, and zinc supplements.[[267]](#cite_note-WHOtreatmentdiarrhoea2005-270)[[268]](#cite_note-BangladeshGuidelinesSeverelyMalnourished-271)[[269]](#cite_note-CDCcholeratraining-272) There is a special oral rehydration solution called ReSoMal which has less sodium and more potassium than standard solution. However, if the diarrhea is severe, the standard solution is preferable as the person needs the extra sodium.[[268]](#cite_note-BangladeshGuidelinesSeverelyMalnourished-271) Obviously, this is a judgment call best made by a physician, and using either solution is better than doing nothing. Zinc supplements often can help reduce the duration and severity of diarrhea, and Vitamin A can also be helpful.[[270]](#cite_note-time-273) The World Health Organization underlines the importance of a person with diarrhea continuing to eat, with a 2005 publication for physicians stating: "Food should *never* be withheld and the child's usual foods should *not* be diluted. Breastfeeding should *always* be continued."[[267]](#cite_note-WHOtreatmentdiarrhoea2005-270)

[Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia") has been pioneering a program that has now become part of the World Bank's prescribed recipe for coping with a food crisis and had been seen by aid organizations as a model of how to best help hungry nations. Through the country's main food assistance program, the Productive Safety Net Program, Ethiopia has been giving rural residents who are chronically short of food, a chance to work for food or cash. Foreign aid organizations like the World Food Program were then able to buy food locally from surplus areas to distribute in areas with a shortage of food.[[271]](#cite_note-274)

The [Green Revolution](/wiki/Green_Revolution "Green Revolution") was widely viewed as an answer to famine in the 1970s and 1980s. Between 1950 and 1984, hybrid strains of high-yielding crops transformed agriculture around the globe and world grain production increased by 250%.[[272]](#cite_note-275) Some[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] criticize the process, stating that these new high-yielding crops require more chemical [fertilizers](/wiki/Fertilizer "Fertilizer") and [pesticides](/wiki/Pesticide "Pesticide"), which can harm the environment.[[273]](#cite_note-276) Although these high-yielding crops make it technically possible to feed more people, there are indications that regional food production has peaked in many world sectors, due to certain strategies associated with intensive agriculture such as [groundwater](/wiki/Groundwater "Groundwater") [overdrafting](/wiki/Overdrafting "Overdrafting") and overuse of [pesticides](/wiki/Pesticide "Pesticide") and other [agricultural chemicals](/wiki/Agricultural_chemicals "Agricultural chemicals").

### Levels of food insecurity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Levels of food insecurity")]

Main article: [Famine scales](/wiki/Famine_scales "Famine scales")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Oxfam_East_Africa_-_A_mass_grave_for_children_in_Dadaab.jpg/250px-Oxfam_East_Africa_-_A_mass_grave_for_children_in_Dadaab.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oxfam_East_Africa_-_A_mass_grave_for_children_in_Dadaab.jpg)

Freshly-dug graves for child victims of the [2011 East Africa drought](/wiki/2011_East_Africa_drought "2011 East Africa drought"), [Dadaab](/wiki/Dadaab "Dadaab") refugee camp, [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya")

In modern times, local and political governments and [non-governmental organizations](/wiki/Non-governmental_organization "Non-governmental organization") that deliver famine relief have limited resources with which to address the multiple situations of food insecurity that are occurring simultaneously. Various methods of categorizing the gradations of food security have thus been used in order to most efficiently allocate food relief. One of the earliest were the [Indian Famine Codes](/wiki/Indian_Famine_Codes "Indian Famine Codes") devised by the British in the 1880s. The Codes listed three stages of food insecurity: near-scarcity, scarcity and famine, and were highly influential in the creation of subsequent famine warning or measurement systems. The early warning system developed to monitor the region inhabited by the [Turkana people](/wiki/Turkana_people "Turkana people") in northern Kenya also has three levels, but links each stage to a pre-planned response to mitigate the crisis and prevent its deterioration

The experiences of famine relief organizations throughout the world over the 1980s and 1990s resulted in at least two major developments: the "livelihoods approach" and the increased use of nutrition indicators to determine the severity of a crisis. Individuals and groups in food stressful situations will attempt to cope by rationing consumption, finding alternative means to supplement income, etc., before taking desperate measures, such as selling off plots of [agricultural](/wiki/Agricultural "Agricultural") land. When all means of self-support are exhausted, the affected population begins to migrate in search of food or fall victim to outright mass [starvation](/wiki/Starvation "Starvation"). Famine may thus be viewed partially as a social phenomenon, involving [markets](/wiki/Market_(economics) "Market (economics)"), the price of food, and social support structures. A second lesson drawn was the increased use of rapid nutrition assessments, in particular of children, to give a quantitative measure of the famine's severity.

Since 2003, many of the most important organizations in famine relief, such as the [World Food Programme](/wiki/World_Food_Programme "World Food Programme") and the [U.S. Agency for International Development](/wiki/U.S._Agency_for_International_Development "U.S. Agency for International Development"), have adopted a five-level scale measuring intensity and magnitude. The intensity scale uses both livelihoods' measures and measurements of mortality and child malnutrition to categorize a situation as food secure, food insecure, food crisis, famine, severe famine, and extreme famine. The number of deaths determines the magnitude designation, with under 1000 fatalities defining a "minor famine" and a "catastrophic famine" resulting in over 1,000,000 deaths.

## Society and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Society and culture")]

Famine personified as an [allegory](/wiki/Allegory "Allegory") is found in some cultures: Famine is one of the [Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse](/wiki/Four_Horsemen_of_the_Apocalypse "Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse") in Christian tradition, a man riding a black horse holding a [balance scale](/wiki/Balance_scale "Balance scale"); the [fear gorta](/wiki/Fear_gorta "Fear gorta") of Irish folklore; or the [Wendigo](/wiki/Wendigo "Wendigo") of [Algonquian](/wiki/Algonquian_peoples "Algonquian peoples") tradition.

### Effects on society

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Famine&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Effects on society")]

A 2023 systematic review and meta-analysis has shown that prenatal famine exposure is associated with an increased risk of mental disorders (such as [depression](/wiki/Major_depressive_disorder "Major depressive disorder") and [schizophrenia](/wiki/Schizophrenia "Schizophrenia")) and changes in [DNA methylation](/wiki/DNA_methylation "DNA methylation"), that is further exacerbating the impact of famine on human health and nutrition.[[274]](#cite_note-277)
